profile.domain: Core Banking Infrastructure Modernization

project_goal.value: Migrate regional bank on-premise data centers to hybrid cloud architecture covering compute, networking, secrets management, and HA failover for transaction processing systems
project_goal.rationale: Core modernization initiative to reduce data center OpEx and improve resilience

target_users.value: Bank infrastructure and platform engineering teams
target_users.rationale: Internal engineering consumers of the new hybrid platform

project_class.rationale: Platform and environment architecture, not a user-facing application

capabilities.rationale: Infrastructure automation, data migration, and batch processing workflows

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-datacenter migration with transaction processing SLAs and hybrid topology

risk_level.rationale: Live transaction systems; outages have regulatory and financial consequences

data_sensitivity.rationale: Transaction processing systems handle payment and settlement data

external_exposure.rationale: Infrastructure platform accessed only by internal engineering teams

access_model.value: Keycloak RBAC with MFA; break-glass emergency access with audit trail
access_model.rationale: Privileged infrastructure access requires strong authentication and full audit

feature_scope.value: Migration wave planning, automated DR drills, hybrid networking, secrets vault, failover orchestration, compliance reporting
feature_scope.rationale: Full scope of the modernization platform

mvp_scope.value: Migration wave 1 (non-production workloads), Vault secrets management, hybrid VPN connectivity, DR drill automation
mvp_scope.rationale: De-risk approach: migrate non-critical workloads first to validate patterns

security_baseline.value: TLS 1.3 everywhere, Vault for secrets, IAM least-privilege, immutable infrastructure, audit logging to SIEM
security_baseline.rationale: Banking security standards for infrastructure access

frontend_stack.value: None - infrastructure project with no browser UI
frontend_stack.rationale: Platform tooling is CLI and Terraform-driven, no browser frontend

backend_stack.value: Go 1.22 with custom service mesh adapters
backend_stack.rationale: Go for performance and low-level networking control in migration tooling

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 (primary) + Oracle 19c (legacy, source of migration)
data_platform.rationale: Dual-platform during migration period; Oracle is legacy source system

hosting_target.value: ECS Fargate + bare-metal Kubernetes hybrid on AWS
hosting_target.rationale: Hybrid topology: cloud-native for new workloads, on-prem K8s for constrained systems

privacy_retention_policy.value: 7-year retention for financial transaction logs; infrastructure audit logs 3 years
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Financial regulatory retention requirements

future_scope.value: Full data center exit, self-service developer platform, FinOps automation
future_scope.rationale: 3-year roadmap after initial migration waves complete

constraints.value: Zero downtime for transaction processing during cutover; Oracle licensing expires in 18 months forcing migration timeline
constraints.rationale: Hard business constraint driving migration urgency

observability_baseline.value: Datadog infrastructure monitoring, distributed tracing across hybrid network, PagerDuty for critical alerts
observability_baseline.rationale: Unified observability across cloud and on-prem is mandatory for migration safety

execution_preference.value: Infrastructure-as-code with Terraform; migration waves in 6-week cycles with rollback checkpoints
execution_preference.rationale: IaC ensures repeatability; wave approach limits blast radius

compliance_context.value: SOC 2 Type II, FFIEC IT Examination Handbook, Basel III operational risk controls
compliance_context.rationale: Bank regulatory obligations for infrastructure changes affecting transaction systems

project.goal: Hybrid cloud migration for transaction processing infrastructure
project.target_users: Infrastructure and platform engineering teams

backend.framework: Custom service mesh adapters + Terraform providers

security.auth: Keycloak with MFA
security.secrets: HashiCorp Vault with AWS KMS backend

open_questions.cutover_strategy: Blue-green vs. traffic shifting for payment systems

reasoner_reviews.product: Migration wave approach correctly de-risks the program. Wave 1 targeting non-production workloads validates network connectivity, Vault integration, and Terraform modules before touching transaction systems. The 18-month constraint from Oracle license expiry creates a hard deadline the team must work backward from.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Hybrid ECS Fargate plus bare-metal Kubernetes topology is architecturally sound for the transition period. The critical design decision is the service mesh adapter layer in Go: it must transparently proxy traffic between on-prem and cloud workloads with sub-millisecond added latency. Recommend Envoy sidecar pattern to avoid reimplementing L7 routing.
reasoner_reviews.security: SOC 2 Type II and FFIEC requirements mandate immutable audit trails for every infrastructure change. HashiCorp Vault with dynamic secrets and lease rotation is the right choice; ensure the Vault cluster itself is HA with 3 nodes minimum and DR replication enabled. Break-glass procedures for production access must be documented and tested quarterly.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Oracle 19c license expiry in 18 months is the primary forcing function. PostgreSQL 16 compatibility layer must be validated against all stored procedures and custom Oracle-specific SQL in the transaction processing codebase before wave 3.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Round 2 plan adequately addressed Vault HA but still lacks a concrete failover_policies schema for the migration wave scheduling system. The data model for migration_waves and service_dependencies needs explicit versioning to support rollback to a prior checkpoint.

backend.service_design: Migration orchestration service in Go exposes gRPC API; wave planner module ingests dependency maps and computes a topologically sorted cutover schedule respecting business unit freeze windows
backend.api_patterns: gRPC for internal orchestration; REST callbacks for GitLab CI webhook integration; Vault API for dynamic secret provisioning
backend.failure_handling: Circuit breaker on Oracle JDBC bridge; automatic checkpoint write before each batch of migrated data_center_nodes; rollback triggers if health check fails post-cutover
backend.scaling_notes: Fargate tasks auto-scale based on active migration_waves count; bare-metal K8s nodes are static capacity managed by cluster-autoscaler with on-prem resource limits
backend.backend_risks: Oracle JDBC bridge latency may bottleneck wave 3 cutover; mitigate by pre-staging transformed data in PostgreSQL before cutover window

security.auth_design: Keycloak with hardware MFA tokens for all privileged infrastructure operations; service accounts use Vault AppRole with short-lived tokens
security.secrets_management: Vault cluster with 3 nodes, AWS KMS auto-unseal, dynamic PostgreSQL credentials with 1-hour leases, secret versioning for rollback
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Every Terraform plan/apply, Vault secret access, and migration wave state change logged to immutable CloudTrail + SIEM; tamper-evident log chain for FFIEC
security.security_risks: Hybrid network boundary increases attack surface; mitigate with microsegmentation and network_segments ACL rules enforced at VPC and bare-metal firewall layers

data.entities: data_center_nodes (id, hostname, dc_zone, workload_type, migration_wave_id, status ENUM pending/migrating/completed/rolled_back), network_segments (id, cidr, zone, acl_rules_json, last_audited_at), vault_secrets (id, path, engine_type, lease_duration, last_rotated_at), failover_policies (id, service_id, rto_seconds, rpo_seconds, trigger_condition, last_tested_at), migration_waves (id, wave_number, start_date, end_date, rollback_checkpoint_s3_key, status), compliance_controls (id, framework, control_id, evidence_s3_key, last_assessed_at), service_dependencies (id, upstream_service_id, downstream_service_id, dependency_type, critical_path_flag)
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 for migration state and compliance_controls; Oracle 19c read-only source during waves 1-3; S3 for checkpoint artifacts and evidence packages
data.migration_notes: Schema compatibility validation tool runs against Oracle stored procedures before each wave; incompatible patterns are flagged for manual porting to PostgreSQL 16 equivalents

devops.deployment_topology: GitLab CI pipeline applies Terraform modules per wave; ECS Fargate for migration orchestration services; bare-metal K8s cluster managed via Rancher for on-prem workloads; Direct Connect for hybrid network
devops.ci_cd_design: GitLab CI: Terraform plan on MR, apply on merge to wave branch; migration wave state machine enforces ordered progression; automated smoke tests post-cutover before wave is marked complete
devops.observability_stack: Datadog Agent on all Fargate tasks and K8s nodes; custom migration_wave_progress dashboard; distributed tracing via Datadog APM across hybrid network segments
devops.rollback_strategy: Each wave writes S3 checkpoint before cutover; automated rollback script re-points DNS and traffic rules back to on-prem within 15 minutes if health checks fail post-cutover
devops.devops_risks: Bare-metal K8s node failures during migration window have no automated replacement; pre-stage spare nodes and document manual recovery runbook

previous_plan.architecture_overview: Hybrid topology with AWS ECS Fargate for cloud-native workloads and bare-metal Kubernetes for constrained on-prem systems connected via Direct Connect.
previous_plan.data_model: data_center_nodes, network_segments, vault_secrets, migration_waves, compliance_controls, service_dependencies - failover_policies entity present but missing RTO/RPO fields.

target_output.title: Regional Bank Hybrid Cloud Migration Platform v3
target_output.executive_summary: A hybrid cloud infrastructure migration platform for a regional bank transitioning transaction processing systems from on-premise data centers to AWS ECS Fargate with a bare-metal Kubernetes bridge. The platform manages migration wave scheduling, HashiCorp Vault HA secrets management, automated DR drills with RTO/RPO attestation, and Oracle-to-PostgreSQL 16 compatibility validation, operating under SOC 2 Type II, FFIEC, and Basel III controls.
target_output.architecture_overview: Hybrid topology: AWS ECS Fargate hosts migration orchestration services and new cloud-native workloads; bare-metal Kubernetes (Rancher-managed) runs constrained on-prem transaction systems during the transition. AWS Direct Connect provides dedicated network connectivity between data centers and the VPC. A Go-based migration orchestration service drives wave progression via a gRPC API, writing state to PostgreSQL 16. HashiCorp Vault 3-node HA cluster with AWS KMS auto-unseal manages all dynamic credentials. Terraform modules codify each wave's infrastructure changes; GitLab CI enforces ordered wave progression with mandatory smoke tests and S3 checkpoint artifacts before marking any wave complete.
target_output.technology_stack: Infrastructure: Terraform 1.8, GitLab CI, AWS Direct Connect, ECS Fargate, bare-metal Kubernetes 1.29 (Rancher). Migration tooling: Go 1.22, Cobra CLI, custom Terraform providers. Secrets: HashiCorp Vault 1.16 with AWS KMS auto-unseal. Databases: PostgreSQL 16 (target), Oracle 19c (legacy source), Redis 7 (session state). Observability: Datadog Agent, distributed tracing, PagerDuty. Compatibility: ora2pg 24 wrapped in Go binary for pre-flight analysis.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Migration Wave Planner: ingests service_dependencies graph, computes topologically sorted cutover schedule respecting business unit freeze windows, outputs wave_schedules with rollback checkpoints. Vault Secrets Engine: dynamic PostgreSQL credentials with 1-hour leases, secret versioning, automated rotation. DR Drill Automation: triggers synthetic failover per failover_policies, measures actual RTO and RPO against configured targets, generates signed attestation_report for risk committee. Oracle Compatibility Scanner: ora2pg-backed analysis of stored procedures, data type mappings, and custom SQL; outputs porting report with severity ratings. Compliance Evidence Collector: assembles SOC 2 and FFIEC control evidence from audit logs, Vault access records, and migration state into S3 evidence packages.
target_output.system_components: Migration Orchestration Service (Go 1.22 on ECS Fargate, gRPC API), Wave State Machine (PostgreSQL 16 state store), Vault HA Cluster (3-node ECS Fargate, AWS KMS auto-unseal, Raft storage), Terraform Module Registry (S3-backed), GitLab CI Runner Pool (ECS Fargate, auto-scaled), Bare-Metal K8s Cluster (Rancher 2.8, on-prem, 12 nodes), Direct Connect Gateway (10Gbps dedicated circuit), Oracle JDBC Bridge (Go adapter on ECS), Oracle Compatibility Scanner (Go binary + ora2pg), Datadog Hybrid Agent (Fargate sidecar + K8s DaemonSet), S3 Checkpoint/Evidence Store.
target_output.workflows: Migration Wave Execution: GitLab CI triggers wave planner for target wave; planner reads service_dependencies, validates no critical_path_flag services are in same wave, writes checkpoint to S3, applies Terraform plan, runs smoke tests, marks wave complete if health checks pass within 15-minute window or triggers automated rollback. DR Drill Procedure: Operator runs drill command specifying service_id; orchestration service reads failover_policies (rto_seconds, rpo_seconds), triggers synthetic network partition, measures time-to-recovery, compares against targets, writes attestation_report_s3_key to failover_policies record, emails risk committee PDF report. Oracle Compatibility Pre-Flight: Wave 3 pre-flight job runs Oracle Compatibility Scanner against all stored procedures, flags UNSUPPORTED items, blocks wave progression if critical incompatibilities remain unresolved, outputs porting_report to S3 for engineering remediation tracking.
target_output.data_model: data_center_nodes (id UUID PK, hostname, dc_zone ENUM us-east/us-west/on-prem, workload_type, migration_wave_id FK, status ENUM pending/migrating/completed/rolled_back, migrated_at). network_segments (id UUID PK, cidr, zone, acl_rules_json, last_audited_at, compliance_verified_flag). vault_secrets (id UUID PK, path, engine_type ENUM database/aws/kv, lease_duration_seconds, last_rotated_at, version). failover_policies (id UUID PK, service_id FK, rto_seconds INTEGER, rpo_seconds INTEGER, trigger_condition, last_tested_at TIMESTAMP, attestation_report_s3_key TEXT, sla_met_flag BOOLEAN). migration_waves (id UUID PK, wave_number INTEGER, start_date, end_date, rollback_checkpoint_s3_key, status ENUM planned/active/complete/rolled_back). compliance_controls (id UUID PK, framework ENUM soc2/ffiec/basel3, control_id, evidence_s3_key, last_assessed_at, status). service_dependencies (id UUID PK, upstream_service_id, downstream_service_id, dependency_type ENUM hard/soft, critical_path_flag BOOLEAN). Core banking financial entities: customer_accounts (id UUID PK, account_number VARCHAR UNIQUE, account_type ENUM checking/savings/money_market, currency CHAR(3), current_balance_cents BIGINT, available_balance_cents BIGINT, status ENUM active/frozen/closed, opened_date DATE), ledger_entries (id UUID PK, account_id FK, transaction_id UUID, entry_type ENUM debit/credit, amount_cents BIGINT, currency CHAR(3), balance_after_cents BIGINT, ledger_date DATE, posting_timestamp TIMESTAMPTZ), settlement_runs (id UUID PK, run_date DATE, network ENUM ach/wire/rtgs, total_transactions INT, total_settlement_amount_cents BIGINT, currency CHAR(3), status ENUM pending/settled/failed, settled_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: gRPC API for internal orchestration: WavePlanner.PlanWave(wave_id) returns schedule, WavePlanner.ExecuteWave(wave_id) triggers cutover, WavePlanner.RollbackWave(wave_id) reverts. DrillService.RunDrill(service_id, scenario) executes DR test and returns attestation. CompatibilityScanner.ScanWave(wave_id) returns porting_report. REST callbacks: POST /webhooks/gitlab (CI pipeline status), POST /vault/lease-renewal (automated lease extension). All endpoints require Vault AppRole token + Keycloak JWT.
target_output.security_and_compliance: SOC 2 Type II: immutable audit trail via CloudTrail + SIEM for all Terraform applies, Vault accesses, and wave state transitions. FFIEC IT Examination Handbook: documented DR procedures with measurable RTO/RPO per failover_policies, quarterly drill cadence with attestation_report evidence. Basel III operational risk: migration risk assessment updated before each wave, residual risk logged to compliance_controls. HashiCorp Vault: 3-node Raft HA, AWS KMS auto-unseal, dynamic PostgreSQL credentials (1-hour leases), secret versioning. Network microsegmentation: network_segments ACL rules enforced at VPC security groups and on-prem firewall. Break-glass access: Keycloak emergency role with automatic CISO page and mandatory session recording.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitLab CI pipeline: Terraform plan on MR with policy-as-code checks (OPA), Terraform apply on merge to wave branch, automated smoke test suite, wave state machine enforces ordered progression. ECS Fargate: migration orchestration service auto-scales on active_wave_count metric. Bare-metal K8s: Rancher 2.8 manages 12-node on-prem cluster; node failure alerts to PagerDuty with manual recovery runbook. Direct Connect: 10Gbps primary + 1Gbps backup circuit. Environments: dev (cloud-only, Terraform workspace), staging (hybrid mirror), prod-wave1 through prod-wave4 (progressively expanded scope). Rollback: S3 checkpoint per wave; DNS and traffic rules revert within 15 minutes on health check failure.
target_output.observability: Datadog Agent on all ECS tasks (sidecar) and K8s nodes (DaemonSet). Custom dashboards: migration_wave_progress (nodes migrated/total, time elapsed, rollback events), vault_lease_health (expiring leases, rotation failures), dr_drill_results (RTO/RPO vs targets per service). Distributed tracing via Datadog APM across hybrid Direct Connect boundary. PagerDuty alerts: migration wave health check failure, Vault unsealing failure, Oracle JDBC bridge latency >500ms, DR drill SLA breach.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: ECS Fargate for migration services scales to zero between waves, minimizing idle cost. Vault cluster: 3x Fargate tasks at ~$150/month total. Direct Connect: $500/month for 10Gbps dedicated circuit. Oracle licensing cost eliminated at wave 3 completion (saving ~$200k/year). Estimated total migration infrastructure: $2,000/month during active waves, dropping to $800/month post-migration.
target_output.phased_implementation: Wave 1 (weeks 1-6): Deploy Vault HA, Terraform modules, GitLab CI pipeline, Direct Connect; migrate non-production workloads; validate hybrid networking and secrets rotation. Wave 2 (weeks 7-12): Migrate internal tooling and batch processing workloads; run first DR drill with RTO/RPO attestation; validate failover_policies data model. Wave 3 (weeks 13-18): Run Oracle Compatibility Scanner; port stored procedures; migrate transaction processing read replicas; validate PostgreSQL 16 compatibility under load. Wave 4 (weeks 19-24): Migrate primary transaction processing; execute final DR drill; decommission Oracle 19c; issue SOC 2 evidence package.
target_output.development_guidelines: All infrastructure changes via Terraform; no manual AWS console modifications. OPA policy-as-code gates on Terraform plans to enforce security baselines. Go migration tooling: 80% unit test coverage, integration tests against local Vault and PostgreSQL. GitLab CI enforces linting (golangci-lint) and Terraform fmt. ADR documents required for wave boundary decisions. Runbooks in repo wiki for every automated operation.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Oracle JDBC bridge latency during wave 3 bottlenecks transaction processing cutover window. Mitigation: pre-stage transformed data in PostgreSQL 16 staging schema before live cutover; reduce wave 3 batch sizes. Risk: bare-metal K8s node failure during active migration wave has no automated replacement path. Mitigation: pre-stage spare nodes; 30-minute manual recovery runbook. Tradeoff: Raft-based Vault HA over Consul reduces external dependency but requires careful node replacement procedures. Tradeoff: ora2pg for compatibility scanning has known gaps with complex Oracle-specific analytic functions; high-complexity stored procedures require manual review.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected blue-green traffic shifting over hard cutover for payment systems to enable instant rollback. Confirmed AWS Direct Connect over VPN for latency guarantees on transaction replication. Oracle 19c will remain read-only source through wave 4, decommissioned only after 30-day parallel-run validation.

metadata.notes: Round 3 revision addressing failover_policies data model completeness and Oracle compatibility validation workflow for banking infrastructure migration.

profile.domain: Developer Sandbox Provisioning for Loan Origination Microservices

project_goal.value: Provision and tear down ephemeral Kubernetes namespace sandboxes for developer testing of loan origination microservices, with seed data injection and cost tagging
project_goal.rationale: Developers need isolated environments to test microservice combinations without affecting shared environments

target_users.value: Backend engineers and QA engineers at a lending technology firm testing loan origination service combinations
target_users.rationale: Primary consumers are developers triggering on-demand sandboxes via CLI

project_class.rationale: CLI-driven provisioning tool with no end-user browser UI

capabilities.rationale: Requires Kubernetes orchestration, GCP integration, batch cleanup jobs, and CLI backend logic

complexity_level.rationale: Kubernetes namespace management and seed data templating add meaningful complexity beyond simple scripting

risk_level.rationale: No customer data in sandboxes but misconfiguration could leak internal service credentials or inflate cloud costs

data_sensitivity.rationale: Sandbox environments contain synthetic loan data and internal service configs, not real customer PII

external_exposure.rationale: Tool runs on internal GKE cluster, accessible only to authenticated engineers on VPN

access_model.value: Custom JWT issued per engineer identity via CI/CD pipeline integration; roles: developer (provision/teardown own sandboxes), ops-admin (teardown any sandbox, manage quotas)
access_model.rationale: Engineers should only manage their own sandboxes unless elevated to ops-admin

feature_scope.value: CLI provisioning of namespaced sandboxes, Kubernetes manifest templating per loan product type, seed data injection from snapshot library, cost tag propagation to GCP billing, Slack notifications on provision/teardown, nightly idle cleanup batch job
feature_scope.rationale: Core features covering the full sandbox lifecycle from provision to automatic expiry

mvp_scope.value: CLI provision and teardown commands for three loan product types (personal, auto, mortgage), seed data injection from pre-built snapshots, nightly cleanup batch job
mvp_scope.rationale: Enough to replace manual kubectl commands and eliminate stale sandbox cost overruns

security_baseline.value: JWT authentication for CLI commands, Kubernetes RBAC preventing cross-namespace access, seed data containing no real customer records, GCP IAM least-privilege service account for provisioning operations
security_baseline.rationale: Internal tool must still enforce namespace isolation to prevent engineers from accidentally accessing each other s sandboxes

frontend_stack.value: None — CLI-only tool with no browser interface
frontend_stack.rationale: Automation tool accessed entirely via command-line; no frontend required

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with Click for CLI framework, kubernetes Python client for cluster operations
backend_stack.rationale: Click provides a mature CLI framework; kubernetes client is the standard Python interface for GKE

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 on Cloud SQL for sandbox registry and provisioning job history
data_platform.rationale: Relational store tracks active sandboxes, their metadata, cost tags, and lifecycle state

hosting_target.value: GKE Autopilot for sandboxes; Cloud SQL for registry; Cloud Run Job for nightly cleanup batch
hosting_target.rationale: GKE Autopilot handles node provisioning automatically; Cloud Run Jobs are cost-effective for scheduled batch workloads

privacy_retention_policy.value: Sandbox environments auto-expire after 8 hours; provisioning logs retained 30 days; no customer data ever injected into sandboxes
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Short TTL minimizes cost and eliminates stale environment risk; log retention supports debugging without compliance burden

future_scope.value: Web dashboard for sandbox status visibility, integration with GitHub PR lifecycle to auto-create sandboxes on PR open, support for additional loan product types
future_scope.rationale: Year 2 roadmap to reduce reliance on CLI for non-engineers

constraints.value: Sandboxes must provision within 90 seconds, budget under $500/month for sandbox infrastructure, no real customer PII in any seed data snapshot
constraints.rationale: 90-second SLA keeps developer flow uninterrupted; cost cap enforces discipline on idle environments

observability_baseline.value: Grafana dashboards with Prometheus metrics for provision latency, active sandbox count, cleanup job success rate; Slack alerting for cleanup failures
observability_baseline.rationale: Lightweight observability appropriate for an internal developer tool

execution_preference.value: Single-team delivery in 6-week sprints; infrastructure as code via Terraform for all GCP resources
execution_preference.rationale: Small team; Terraform ensures repeatable infrastructure across dev and prod GCP projects

project.goal: CLI tool to provision ephemeral Kubernetes namespaces for loan origination microservice testing
project.target_users: Lending platform backend and QA engineers

backend.framework: Click CLI + kubernetes Python client
backend.api: Internal subprocess and Kubernetes API calls, no HTTP API surface

security.auth: Custom JWT per engineer identity
security.seed_data: Synthetic loan data only, no real PII

devops.hosting: GKE Autopilot + Cloud SQL + Cloud Run Jobs

constraints.pii_policy: No real customer data in any sandbox

reasoner_reviews.product: The 90-second provision SLA is achievable with pre-built namespace template manifests and snapshot-based seed injection — avoid dynamic schema migrations at provision time. The three MVP loan product types (personal, auto, mortgage) cover the core microservice topologies engineers need to test integration contracts.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Python Click plus kubernetes client is the right choice for this team. Namespace template rendering via Jinja2 will be faster than Helm chart generation for sub-90-second provisioning. Store sandbox state in Cloud SQL rather than Kubernetes CRDs to keep query and reporting simple.
reasoner_reviews.security: Sandbox namespaces must have Kubernetes NetworkPolicy restricting cross-namespace communication. The JWT validation must verify engineer identity against an allowlist synced from internal LDAP to prevent credential sharing. Ensure seed data snapshots are periodically audited to confirm no real PII has been accidentally included.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Budget of $500/month is tight but achievable given GKE Autopilot pricing — enforce resource quotas per namespace (CPU: 2 cores, memory: 4Gi) to prevent runaway costs. Nightly cleanup must run reliably; if Cloud Run Job fails, a fallback GitHub Actions scheduled workflow should trigger the same cleanup script.

backend.service_design: Single Python package with three Click command groups: 'sandbox' (provision, teardown, status, list), 'snapshots' (list, refresh), 'admin' (quota-check, force-teardown). Each command group maps to a service module that calls the Kubernetes API and updates Cloud SQL state.
backend.business_modules: NamespaceProvisioner: renders Jinja2 namespace manifest templates, applies resource quotas, injects NetworkPolicy. SeedDataInjector: selects appropriate snapshot by loan product type, restores PostgreSQL snapshot into the sandbox namespace DB sidecar. CostTagger: applies GCP billing labels to all sandbox resources via Resource Manager API. LifecycleManager: tracks TTL in Cloud SQL, triggers teardown when expired.
backend.background_jobs: Nightly cleanup Cloud Run Job: queries sandbox_environments for entries where created_at is older than 8 hours, posts Slack warning, then calls kubernetes client to delete namespaces and updates DB status to deprovisioned. Job runs at 02:00 UTC.
backend.backend_risks: Kubernetes API rate limiting if many engineers provision simultaneously; mitigate with a provisioning queue backed by Cloud Tasks. Seed data snapshot stale risk if loan microservice schemas drift without snapshot refresh.

security.auth_design: JWT tokens issued via a lightweight token endpoint called by GitHub Actions OIDC token exchange. Engineers authenticate using GitHub identity; token includes engineer_id and role claims. Token TTL: 4 hours.
security.authorization_model: Developer role: can provision/teardown/status their own sandboxes enforced by engineer_id claim match on sandbox_environments record. Ops-admin role: unrestricted sandbox management plus quota-check and force-teardown commands.
security.abuse_prevention: Per-engineer quota: max 2 concurrent sandboxes. Per-namespace resource quota: 2 CPU, 4Gi memory. Kubernetes NetworkPolicy blocks inter-namespace pod communication to prevent sandbox cross-contamination.
security.security_risks: Seed data snapshot pipeline must enforce snapshots are generated from synthetic data generators, not from production DB exports. Risk of JWT token reuse if engineers share tokens; mitigate with short TTL and token binding to engineer_id.

data.entities: sandbox_environments (id UUID PK, engineer_id, loan_product_type ENUM personal/auto/mortgage, namespace_name, status ENUM provisioning/active/teardown/deprovisioned, created_at, expires_at, seed_snapshot_id FK). provisioning_jobs (id UUID PK, sandbox_id FK, action ENUM provision/teardown, started_at, completed_at, outcome ENUM success/failed, error_message). seed_data_snapshots (id UUID PK, loan_product_type, gcs_path, created_at, size_bytes, is_current BOOLEAN). cost_tags (id UUID PK, sandbox_id FK, gcp_label_key, gcp_label_value, applied_at). namespace_configs (id UUID PK, loan_product_type, manifest_template_path, service_list JSONB, resource_quota_json JSONB).
data.storage_design: Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 for sandbox registry. Seed data snapshots stored as pg_dump files in GCS with versioned prefix per loan product type. Namespace manifest templates stored in GCS bucket, loaded at provision time.
data.retention_and_deletion: sandbox_environments records retained 30 days after deprovisioning for audit. provisioning_jobs logs retained 30 days. seed_data_snapshots retained until superseded by a newer snapshot.

devops.deployment_topology: CLI tool package published to internal PyPI; engineers install via pip. A Cloud Run Job image runs the cleanup batch. Kubernetes manifests for NetworkPolicy and ResourceQuota templates stored in GCS and pulled at runtime.
devops.environments: sandbox-dev GCP project: engineers GKE Autopilot cluster. sandbox-prod GCP project: cleanup job and JWT token endpoint. Both projects managed via Terraform.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: flake8 + mypy lint, pytest unit tests (80% coverage gate), Docker build and push to Artifact Registry, trigger Cloud Run Job deployment. CLI package published to internal PyPI on semver tag push.
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus sidecar scrapes provision latency histogram, active_sandboxes gauge, cleanup_job_success counter. Grafana dashboard with panels for active sandboxes by engineer, provision p95 latency, cleanup failures. Slack webhook alert on cleanup job failure.
devops.devops_risks: GKE Autopilot cold-start for new node provisioning can add 60-90 seconds, risking the 90-second SLA. Maintain a warm pool of 2 reserved nodes. Cloud Run Job quota limits if cleanup job is triggered manually and scheduled run overlaps.

target_output.thinking_summary: This is a Python CLI automation tool for Kubernetes sandbox provisioning — no frontend agent needed. The key challenge is meeting the 90-second provision SLA, which requires pre-built namespace templates and snapshot-based seed injection rather than dynamic provisioning. Cost control is critical given the $500/month budget; namespace resource quotas and nightly cleanup are the primary levers. Seed data must never contain real PII, which requires snapshot generation pipeline guardrails.
target_output.title: Loan Origination Sandbox Provisioner Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A Python CLI automation tool that provisions and tears down ephemeral GKE Autopilot namespaces for testing loan origination microservices. Engineers invoke CLI commands to spin up isolated namespaces with seed data for personal, auto, or mortgage loan product topologies within 90 seconds. A nightly Cloud Run Job deprovisions idle sandboxes to enforce cost budgets. The tool tracks sandbox lifecycle state in Cloud SQL PostgreSQL and applies GCP billing labels for cost attribution.
target_output.architecture_overview: CLI-only architecture: Python Click application invoked by engineers on workstations or in CI pipelines. On provision, the CLI authenticates via JWT (GitHub OIDC exchange), renders a Jinja2 namespace manifest for the requested loan product type, applies it to GKE Autopilot via the kubernetes Python client, injects seed data from a GCS-backed pg_dump snapshot into the namespace DB sidecar, applies GCP billing labels via Resource Manager API, and writes a sandbox_environments record to Cloud SQL. On teardown, the reverse process runs. A Cloud Run Job executes nightly cleanup independently of the CLI, querying Cloud SQL for expired sandboxes and deleting their namespaces. No HTTP API surface is exposed; all operations are driven by CLI commands or the scheduled batch job.
target_output.technology_stack: CLI: Python 3.12, Click 8.1, kubernetes Python client 29.x, Jinja2 for manifest templating. Database: Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 for sandbox registry. Storage: GCS for seed data pg_dump snapshots and manifest templates. Compute: GKE Autopilot for sandboxes, Cloud Run Jobs for nightly cleanup. Auth: GitHub OIDC to custom JWT token endpoint (Cloud Run service). CI/CD: GitHub Actions. Observability: Prometheus pushgateway + Grafana. Infrastructure as code: Terraform for all GCP resources.
target_output.functional_feature_map: sandbox provision: creates namespace with NetworkPolicy, ResourceQuota, and injected seed data for specified loan product type (personal/auto/mortgage). sandbox teardown: deletes namespace and marks record deprovisioned in registry. sandbox status/list: queries Cloud SQL for active sandboxes by engineer. snapshots list/refresh: manages seed data snapshot library in GCS. admin quota-check: reports per-engineer sandbox usage against limits. admin force-teardown: ops-admin override for any sandbox. Nightly cleanup batch: scans expired sandboxes, posts Slack warnings, deprovisions, logs outcomes to provisioning_jobs.
target_output.system_components: GKE Autopilot cluster (sandbox namespaces with NetworkPolicy and ResourceQuota per namespace). Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 (sandbox registry tables). GCS bucket (seed pg_dump snapshots and namespace manifest Jinja2 templates). Cloud Run Job (nightly cleanup at 02:00 UTC). Custom JWT token endpoint (Cloud Run service, GitHub OIDC exchange). Artifact Registry (Cloud Run Job Docker image). Internal PyPI (CLI package distribution). Prometheus pushgateway + Grafana (observability).
target_output.workflows: Sandbox Provision: Engineer runs 'sandbox provision --product mortgage --alias test-apr'. CLI exchanges GitHub OIDC token for JWT, checks engineer has fewer than 2 active sandboxes, renders mortgage namespace manifest from GCS template, calls kubernetes client to create namespace with ResourceQuota (2 CPU, 4Gi) and NetworkPolicy, retrieves current mortgage seed snapshot path from Cloud SQL, runs pg_restore into namespace DB sidecar, applies GCP billing labels (engineer_id, loan_product, team), inserts sandbox_environments record with expires_at = now + 8h, prints connection strings to stdout. Total target: under 90 seconds. Nightly Cleanup: Cloud Run Job queries sandbox_environments WHERE expires_at is past and status is active, posts Slack message listing engineers and sandbox names to be deprovisioned, calls kubernetes client delete_namespace for each, updates status to deprovisioned, logs outcomes to provisioning_jobs, emits cleanup_job_success counter. On failure, posts Slack alert and exits non-zero triggering Cloud Run retry.
target_output.data_model: sandbox_environments (id UUID PK, engineer_id VARCHAR, loan_product_type ENUM personal/auto/mortgage, namespace_name VARCHAR UNIQUE, status ENUM provisioning/active/teardown/deprovisioned, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ, expires_at TIMESTAMPTZ, seed_snapshot_id UUID FK). provisioning_jobs (id UUID PK, sandbox_id UUID FK, action ENUM provision/teardown/cleanup, started_at TIMESTAMPTZ, completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ, outcome ENUM success/failed, error_message TEXT). seed_data_snapshots (id UUID PK, loan_product_type ENUM, gcs_path VARCHAR, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ, size_bytes BIGINT, is_current BOOLEAN). cost_tags (id UUID PK, sandbox_id UUID FK, gcp_label_key VARCHAR, gcp_label_value VARCHAR, applied_at TIMESTAMPTZ). namespace_configs (id UUID PK, loan_product_type ENUM UNIQUE, manifest_template_gcs_path VARCHAR, service_list JSONB, resource_quota_json JSONB, updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: No HTTP API surface. Internal Python module interfaces: NamespaceProvisioner.provision(engineer_id, loan_product_type, alias) returns SandboxRecord. NamespaceProvisioner.teardown(sandbox_id, requester_id) returns None. SeedDataInjector.inject(namespace_name, loan_product_type) returns None. CostTagger.tag(sandbox_id, gcp_project, namespace_name) returns None. LifecycleManager.list_expired() returns List of SandboxRecord. SandboxRegistry.get_active_by_engineer(engineer_id) returns List of SandboxRecord. CLI commands map 1:1 to these service module methods.
target_output.security_and_compliance: JWT authentication via GitHub OIDC exchange; tokens carry engineer_id and role claims with 4-hour TTL. Per-engineer quota enforced at provision time (max 2 concurrent sandboxes). Kubernetes NetworkPolicy in each sandbox namespace blocks all ingress from other namespaces. Kubernetes RBAC: provisioner service account has namespace-scoped create/delete; no cluster-admin. Seed data snapshot generation pipeline enforces all snapshots are produced by a synthetic data generator (Faker-based loan records), not production exports — enforced by CI gate that rejects snapshot files containing real SSN or account number patterns via regex scan. GCP IAM: Cloud Run Job service account has Cloud SQL Client and GKE Namespace Admin roles only.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Terraform manages: GKE Autopilot cluster, Cloud SQL instance, GCS buckets (snapshots, templates), Cloud Run services (JWT endpoint, cleanup job), VPC with private IP for Cloud SQL, IAM bindings. GitHub Actions pipeline: flake8 + mypy lint, pytest unit tests (80% coverage gate), Docker build and push to Artifact Registry, update Cloud Run Job image, publish CLI to internal PyPI on semver tag push. Environments: sandbox-dev GCP project (engineer testing), sandbox-prod GCP project (production cleanup job, JWT endpoint). Rollback: Cloud Run Job image pinned by digest; redeploy previous digest on failure. Warm node pool: 2 reserved GKE Autopilot nodes pre-provisioned to avoid cold-start latency on first provision of the day.
target_output.observability: Prometheus metrics emitted by CLI via pushgateway: provision_latency_seconds histogram (labels: loan_product_type, outcome), active_sandboxes gauge (label: loan_product_type), cleanup_job_success_total counter. Grafana dashboard panels: active sandbox count by engineer (table), provision p95 latency by product type (line chart), cleanup success/failure rate (stat). Slack webhook alert fires on cleanup job failure and when active_sandboxes exceeds 20 (budget risk threshold). provisioning_jobs table serves as structured audit log for all lifecycle events.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Target cost under $500/month. GKE Autopilot charges per pod resource request: 2 CPU + 4Gi per sandbox at roughly $0.08/hour per sandbox. At 20 concurrent sandboxes maximum with 8-hour average lifetime: $0.08 * 20 * 8h = $12.80/day peak, roughly $384/month. Cloud SQL micro instance: ~$30/month. Cloud Run Jobs: negligible. Nightly cleanup is the primary cost control lever; Grafana alert fires before budget is breached.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (4 weeks): sandbox provision/teardown/status/list commands for personal, auto, mortgage loan products; static Jinja2 manifest templates; seed data injection from GCS snapshots; Cloud SQL sandbox registry; nightly cleanup Cloud Run Job; GitHub Actions CI pipeline; Prometheus + Grafana observability; internal PyPI package publish. Phase 2 (2 weeks): admin quota-check and force-teardown commands; GCP billing label propagation; Slack notifications on provision/teardown events; snapshot refresh CLI command integrated with synthetic data generator.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python 3.12 strict typing with mypy (no untyped defs allowed in CI). Click commands decorated with require_auth middleware that validates JWT and populates RequestContext. Unit tests use pytest with kubernetes client mocked via unittest.mock; Cloud SQL interactions tested against local PostgreSQL fixture. 80% coverage gate in CI. All GCP API calls wrapped in thin client adapters to allow test injection. Seed data snapshot generator maintained in a separate package to enforce separation from provisioning logic.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: GKE Autopilot cold-start adds 60-90 seconds to provision time, breaking the 90-second SLA on first morning provision. Mitigation: maintain 2 warm reserved nodes via a keep-alive sandbox replaced immediately on teardown. Risk: Seed data snapshot drift if loan microservice DB schema changes without regenerating snapshots, causing pg_restore failures at provision time. Mitigation: snapshot refresh job runs weekly in CI and provision command validates snapshot schema version against current microservice migration version. Tradeoff: no HTTP API surface simplifies security posture but limits integration with future web dashboard — service module interfaces are designed for easy FastAPI wrapping.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Chose Cloud Run Job over Kubernetes CronJob for nightly cleanup because Cloud Run provides simpler retry semantics and cost isolation. Confirmed pg_dump/pg_restore over logical replication for seed injection given the short-lived nature of sandboxes. Decided against Helm charts in favor of Jinja2 templates to minimize dependency and keep provision time under SLA.

metadata.notes: Sandbox provisioner for loan origination microservices testing on GKE Autopilot with nightly cleanup batch.

profile.domain: Internal Microservices Client SDK for Retail Banking

project_goal.value: Python SDK providing standardized client interfaces for an internal microservices mesh in a retail bank, abstracting service discovery, circuit breaking, and distributed tracing so product teams avoid duplicating boilerplate across dozens of services
project_goal.rationale: Each product squad reimplements retry logic and service discovery independently, causing inconsistent failure behavior across 30+ services

target_users.value: Backend Python engineers across product squads (payments, lending, identity, cards) building microservices on the internal mesh
target_users.rationale: Primary consumers are service authors who import the SDK as a dependency

project_class.rationale: Distributed as a Python package on internal PyPI, not a hosted service

capabilities.rationale: SDK provides backend service client logic, Redis-backed circuit breaker state, and integrations with service registry and tracing infrastructure

complexity_level.rationale: Distributed tracing context propagation, circuit breaker state machine, and service registry auto-discovery require careful async-safe implementation

risk_level.rationale: SDK is imported into every service; a bug in retry logic or circuit breaker could cascade failures across the entire mesh

data_sensitivity.rationale: SDK handles service-to-service call metadata; no direct customer data flows through the SDK itself

external_exposure.rationale: SDK published to internal PyPI only; not available outside the bank network

access_model.value: No end-user access model; consuming services authenticate with their own service JWT to the registry. SDK reads service identity from environment variable BANK_SERVICE_ID at import time.
access_model.rationale: SDK is a library, not a service; authentication is the responsibility of the consuming service

feature_scope.value: Auto-discovery of healthy service endpoints from internal service registry, circuit breaker with open/half-open/closed state machine, distributed tracing via OpenTelemetry context injection, configurable retry policies with exponential backoff, structured fallback exceptions with retry-after metadata, SDK version manifest reporting
feature_scope.rationale: These are the exact boilerplate patterns that squads currently reimplement inconsistently

mvp_scope.value: Service discovery client, circuit breaker with Redis-backed state, OpenTelemetry trace context injection, retry policy with exponential backoff and jitter
mvp_scope.rationale: These four features eliminate the most duplicated boilerplate across squad codebases

security_baseline.value: TLS 1.3 for all service-to-service calls made through the SDK, JWT propagation headers auto-injected from request context, no secrets stored in SDK configuration
security_baseline.rationale: SDK must enforce transport security and identity propagation consistently without requiring each team to configure it manually

frontend_stack.value: None — Python library package with no browser interface
frontend_stack.rationale: Library SDK has no UI component

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with httpx for async HTTP, tenacity for retry logic, opentelemetry-sdk for tracing
backend_stack.rationale: httpx supports both sync and async; tenacity provides composable retry decorators; opentelemetry is the internal standard for distributed tracing

data_platform.value: Redis 7 for circuit breaker state storage shared across service instances
data_platform.rationale: Circuit breaker state must be shared across all instances of a service to avoid split-brain open/closed state

hosting_target.value: Published to internal PyPI (Artifactory); no hosted runtime
hosting_target.rationale: Library SDK is distributed as a package, not deployed as a service

privacy_retention_policy.value: SDK logs only service IDs and trace spans — no customer data. Trace data retained per Datadog retention policy (15 days hot). No PII passes through SDK internals.
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: SDK is an infrastructure library; it must not become a data sink

future_scope.value: Support for gRPC service calls in addition to HTTP, adaptive circuit breaker thresholds based on Datadog SLO metrics, Go language port for non-Python squads
future_scope.rationale: Go services on the mesh need the same standardization

constraints.value: SDK import must add under 50ms to service startup time; zero transitive dependency conflicts with FastAPI or Django; must work in Python 3.11 and 3.12
constraints.rationale: Services run on both Python versions; startup latency budget is tight in containerized deployments

observability_baseline.value: SDK emits OpenTelemetry spans for every outbound call; circuit breaker state transitions logged as structured events; Datadog integration via OTEL exporter
observability_baseline.rationale: Squads must be able to observe SDK behavior in their existing Datadog dashboards without additional instrumentation

execution_preference.value: Platform team owns SDK; semver versioning with deprecation notices; changelog-driven releases; consuming squads pin to minor versions
execution_preference.rationale: Breaking changes must be communicated with migration guides; squads cannot be surprised by API changes

project.goal: Standardized Python SDK for internal microservices mesh with service discovery, circuit breaking, and distributed tracing
project.target_users: Backend Python engineers at retail bank product squads

backend.framework: httpx + tenacity + opentelemetry-sdk
backend.api: Python package API surface, no HTTP endpoints

security.auth: JWT propagation from request context
security.transport: TLS 1.3 enforced for all SDK-initiated calls

constraints.dependency_conflicts: No conflicts with FastAPI or Django

reasoner_reviews.product: The four MVP features (service discovery, circuit breaker, OTEL tracing, retry policy) are the right prioritization — they address the highest-pain boilerplate. Version manifest reporting should be included in MVP so platform team can track which SDK version is running across the mesh.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Redis-backed circuit breaker state is the correct choice for a shared state requirement, but the SDK must handle Redis unavailability gracefully — if Redis is down, the circuit breaker should default to closed (allow through) rather than blocking all calls. The service registry client should cache endpoint lists locally with a TTL to avoid a registry outage cascading into a mesh-wide failure.
reasoner_reviews.security: JWT propagation from request context must extract the token from the incoming request context variable, not from environment variables, to avoid cross-request token leakage in async frameworks. The SDK must validate that TLS 1.3 is negotiated before sending any request, rejecting downgrades silently.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 50ms startup latency constraint means lazy initialization is required — the registry client must not resolve endpoints at import time. All connections (Redis, registry) should be established on first use and pooled thereafter.

backend.service_design: Single Python package 'bank-mesh-sdk' with three public modules: bank_mesh.client (BankServiceClient class), bank_mesh.circuit_breaker (CircuitBreaker class with Redis backend), bank_mesh.tracing (TraceContext utilities). All modules support both sync and async usage patterns.
backend.api_patterns: BankServiceClient.get(service_name, path, **kwargs) and BankServiceClient.post(service_name, path, body, **kwargs) as primary API. Circuit breaker applied transparently. Fallback exception BankServiceUnavailableError raised with retry_after_seconds attribute when circuit is open.
backend.failure_handling: Circuit breaker transitions: closed (normal operation) to open (error rate exceeds threshold) after 5 failures in 30-second window. Half-open: single probe request after cool-down period (default 60 seconds). If probe succeeds, transition back to closed; if fails, remain open. Redis HASH stores state per (caller_service, target_service) pair.
backend.backend_risks: Async context variable propagation for JWT tokens can fail in background tasks that lose the request context. Mitigation: document that background tasks must explicitly pass token as parameter rather than relying on context variable injection.

security.auth_design: SDK reads JWT from contextvars.ContextVar populated by the consuming service middleware. Injects as Authorization: Bearer header on every outbound call. Never reads from environment variables to prevent cross-request leakage in async request handlers.
security.authorization_model: No authorization logic in SDK itself. SDK is responsible only for propagating the caller service identity JWT. Authorization decisions are made by the target service.
security.security_risks: If consuming service fails to set the JWT context variable, outbound calls will be made without authentication headers. SDK should emit a warning log when no JWT is found in context rather than silently proceeding.

data.entities: service_registry_entries (service_name, endpoint_url, health_status ENUM healthy/unhealthy/draining, last_checked_at, weight). circuit_breaker_states (caller_service, target_service, state ENUM closed/open/half_open, failure_count, last_failure_at, opened_at, next_probe_at stored as Redis HASH). retry_policies (service_name, max_attempts, backoff_base_seconds, backoff_max_seconds, jitter BOOLEAN, retryable_status_codes LIST stored as JSON config). sdk_version_manifests (version, released_at, changelog_url, min_python_version).
data.storage_design: Redis 7 HASH keys for circuit breaker state with TTL equal to 2x cool-down period to auto-expire stale state. Service registry entries cached in process memory as a dict with 30-second TTL; refreshed asynchronously in background thread.
data.consistency_notes: Redis circuit breaker state uses optimistic locking via WATCH/MULTI/EXEC to prevent race conditions when multiple service instances update state simultaneously.

devops.deployment_topology: No runtime deployment. Package published to internal Artifactory PyPI on every semver tag. GitHub Actions workflow: pytest + coverage, mypy strict, publish to Artifactory.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: mypy strict type checking, pytest with 90% coverage gate, tox matrix for Python 3.11 and 3.12, publish to Artifactory on tag push. Breaking change detection via griffe diff in CI.
devops.devops_risks: Internal PyPI availability is a hard dependency for service deployments that pin SDK version. Ensure Artifactory is in the service mesh SLA. SDK version pins in consuming services can drift and create upgrade debt.

target_output.title: Bank Mesh Client SDK Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A Python package providing standardized service-to-service call infrastructure for retail bank microservices. Distributed via internal Artifactory PyPI, the SDK gives product squad services auto-discovery of healthy endpoints from the internal service registry, circuit breaking with Redis-backed shared state, OpenTelemetry trace context injection into every outbound call, and configurable retry policies with exponential backoff and jitter. Squads import bank-mesh-sdk as a dependency and call BankServiceClient rather than implementing httpx retry logic from scratch.
target_output.architecture_overview: Package architecture with no hosted runtime. The SDK is a Python package imported by consuming services at startup. On first call, BankServiceClient lazily resolves healthy endpoints for the target service from the internal registry (cached in-process with 30-second TTL). Outbound calls are made via httpx with JWT injected from the caller request contextvars. CircuitBreaker evaluates error rate against a Redis HASH keyed by (caller_service, target_service); state transitions (closed/open/half-open) are written back to Redis using WATCH/MULTI/EXEC optimistic locking. OpenTelemetry spans are created for each outbound call and exported to Datadog via OTLP exporter. The SDK adds under 50ms to service startup via lazy initialization. No hosted endpoints, no container images, no deployment topology.
target_output.technology_stack: Python 3.12 (compatible with 3.11). httpx 0.27 for async and sync HTTP. tenacity 8.x for retry policy composition. opentelemetry-sdk 1.x with opentelemetry-exporter-otlp for Datadog. redis-py 5.x for circuit breaker state. Pydantic v2 for configuration validation. tox for multi-Python test matrix. griffe for breaking change detection. Published to internal Artifactory PyPI. CI via GitHub Actions.
target_output.functional_feature_map: BankServiceClient: resolves target service endpoints from registry, injects JWT and trace headers, applies retry policy, delegates to CircuitBreaker. CircuitBreaker: evaluates per-(caller, target) error rate in Redis, transitions state machine, raises BankServiceUnavailableError with retry_after_seconds when open. TraceContext: creates OTEL spans per outbound call, propagates W3C traceparent header. RetryPolicy: configurable max_attempts, exponential backoff with jitter, retryable HTTP status code list. ServiceRegistry: caches endpoint lists per service name with TTL, refreshes asynchronously. VersionManifest: exposes sdk.__version__ and reports to a registry endpoint on import for mesh-wide SDK version telemetry.
target_output.system_components: bank-mesh-sdk Python package (published to Artifactory). BankServiceClient module (httpx-based, sync+async). CircuitBreaker module (Redis HASH state machine). TraceContext module (OTEL span creation and W3C header injection). ServiceRegistry module (in-process endpoint cache). RetryPolicy configuration (Pydantic model). Internal service registry (existing infrastructure, not owned by SDK). Redis 7 cluster (existing infrastructure, consumed by SDK for circuit breaker state). Datadog OTLP endpoint (existing infrastructure, SDK exports traces to it).
target_output.workflows: Outbound Service Call: Consuming service calls BankServiceClient.post('payments-service', '/v1/charge', body). SDK reads JWT from contextvars, checks CircuitBreaker state for (caller, payments-service) from Redis — if open, raises BankServiceUnavailableError immediately. If closed, resolves healthy endpoint from ServiceRegistry cache, creates OTEL span, injects Authorization and traceparent headers into httpx request, executes call with tenacity retry policy. On 5xx response, increments Redis error counter; if threshold breached, transitions circuit to open state in Redis. Records span outcome. Circuit Breaker Recovery: After cool-down period, CircuitBreaker sets state to half_open in Redis and allows one probe request. If probe returns 2xx, transitions to closed and resets error counter. If probe returns error, extends open state by another cool-down period and logs structured event.
target_output.data_model: Redis HASH schema for circuit breaker (key: cb:{caller_service}:{target_service}, fields: state ENUM closed/open/half_open, failure_count INT, last_failure_at ISO8601, opened_at ISO8601, next_probe_at ISO8601; TTL: 2x cool-down period). In-process service registry cache (dict keyed by service_name, value: list of ServiceEndpoint(url, health_status, weight, cached_at)). RetryPolicy config (Pydantic model: service_name, max_attempts INT default 3, backoff_base FLOAT default 0.5s, backoff_max FLOAT default 30s, jitter BOOL default True, retryable_codes LIST default [429,500,502,503,504]). sdk_version_manifests (read-only list of released versions fetched from Artifactory API for version telemetry reporting).
target_output.api_design: Python public API surface: BankServiceClient(service_id: str, redis_url: str, registry_url: str) constructor. client.get(target_service: str, path: str, **httpx_kwargs) -> httpx.Response (async and sync variants). client.post(target_service: str, path: str, body: dict, **httpx_kwargs) -> httpx.Response. CircuitBreaker.call(func: Callable, caller: str, target: str) -> Any. BankServiceUnavailableError(message: str, target_service: str, retry_after_seconds: int). TraceContext.inject_headers(headers: dict) -> dict. configure_retry_policy(service_name: str, policy: RetryPolicy) for per-service overrides. Note: this section still incorrectly references an internal admin HTTP endpoint GET /sdk/health for SDK introspection — this is a contract violation carried forward intentionally to demonstrate a flawed plan.
target_output.security_and_compliance: JWT propagation: SDK reads token from contextvars.ContextVar set by consuming service request middleware — never from environment variables — to prevent cross-request token leakage in async ASGI apps. TLS 1.3 enforced by httpx SSL context; SSL verification uses the internal CA bundle path from BANK_CA_BUNDLE env var. No secrets stored in SDK configuration; Redis URL and registry URL passed as constructor arguments. OTEL spans never record request body content, only service name, path, HTTP method, and status code. SOC 2 Type II internal controls satisfied by structured audit logging of circuit breaker state transitions.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Package release workflow: engineer opens PR with semver bump in pyproject.toml. GitHub Actions runs tox matrix (Python 3.11, 3.12), mypy strict, griffe diff for breaking change detection (fails CI if public API changes without major version bump). On merge to main with semver tag, Actions builds sdist and wheel and publishes to Artifactory internal PyPI. Consuming services update their pinned version in requirements.txt and run their own test suites. No containers, no Fargate, no infrastructure provisioning required for SDK releases. Changelog maintained in CHANGELOG.md with migration guides for minor and major version bumps.
target_output.observability: OTEL spans exported to Datadog via OTLP exporter: span attributes include bank.service.caller, bank.service.target, bank.circuit_breaker.state, http.status_code, http.method. Circuit breaker state transitions logged as structured JSON events (transition, caller_service, target_service, failure_count, new_state, timestamp). Datadog dashboard for platform team: circuit breaker open rate by service pair (line chart), SDK version distribution across mesh (bar chart from version manifest reports), p99 outbound call latency by target service (heat map).
target_output.cost_and_scaling: No infrastructure cost for the SDK itself — it is a package, not a hosted service. Redis cost is shared with existing Redis clusters in consuming service deployments; circuit breaker adds negligible key count (one HASH per service pair per service instance). Artifactory storage for package versions is minimal. Scaling behavior: SDK is in-process so it scales with each consuming service instance automatically.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 v1.0 (6 weeks): BankServiceClient with service registry integration, CircuitBreaker with Redis state machine, OpenTelemetry trace context injection, RetryPolicy with tenacity, tox CI matrix, Artifactory publish workflow, migration guide for first three pilot squads (payments, identity, lending). Phase 2 v1.1 (3 weeks): per-service RetryPolicy overrides, ServiceRegistry background refresh with configurable TTL, VersionManifest reporting, Datadog dashboard for platform team. Phase 3 v2.0 (future): gRPC transport support alongside HTTP, adaptive circuit breaker thresholds from SLO metrics feed.
target_output.development_guidelines: Strict type annotations with mypy --strict; no Any types in public API surface. All public classes and methods documented with docstrings including parameter types and exception types. pytest with 90% coverage gate; unit tests mock Redis and httpx at the transport layer. tox runs tests on Python 3.11 and 3.12. griffe diff in CI blocks merge if public API changes without major version bump. Changelog entry required for every PR. Avoid importing from consuming service frameworks (no FastAPI or Django imports in SDK core).
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Redis unavailability causes circuit breaker to fail open or fail closed depending on error handling policy. Mitigation: SDK defaults to fail-open (allow calls through) when Redis is unreachable, logging a warning, to prevent Redis outage from cascading into mesh-wide call blocking. Risk: In-process service registry cache can serve stale endpoints after a service instance drains. Mitigation: 30-second TTL with background refresh; consuming services should configure health-check-aware endpoint registration in the registry. Tradeoff: Redis shared state for circuit breaker adds a network hop versus pure in-memory state; this is required to prevent split-brain behavior across scaled service instances.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed Redis HASH over PostgreSQL for circuit breaker state due to low-latency read requirement on every outbound call. Chose tenacity over custom retry implementation to leverage battle-tested backoff and jitter logic. Confirmed lazy initialization over eager startup to meet the 50ms import overhead constraint.

metadata.notes: Intentionally flawed round 2 plan: api_design section still contains one hosted HTTP endpoint reference violating library_sdk project class constraint, demonstrating incomplete fix of ISS-001.

profile.domain: Internal Banking Microservices API Developer Portal

project_goal.value: Static developer portal documenting the internal banking microservices API catalogue, including service ownership, SLA tiers, versioning policy, and interactive OpenAPI specs for engineers across product squads
project_goal.rationale: Squads lack a single source of truth for which services exist, who owns them, and what their SLA commitments are

target_users.value: Backend and frontend engineers across product squads (payments, lending, identity, cards) who need to discover and integrate with internal services
target_users.rationale: Primary users are engineers browsing service documentation and downloading client stubs

project_class.rationale: Pure static site — all content generated at build time from OpenAPI specs and service catalogue YAML files; no server-side runtime

capabilities.rationale: Static frontend delivery with embedded analytics for page view and search tracking; no backend runtime or database

complexity_level.rationale: Static site generation with CI publishing pipeline is well-understood; no runtime complexity

risk_level.rationale: Internal-only documentation site with no customer data, no write operations, and SSO authentication via header injection at CDN

data_sensitivity.rationale: Service catalogue contains internal API specs and squad ownership information, not customer data

external_exposure.rationale: Portal is accessible only on the internal network behind Okta SSO header injection at CloudFront

access_model.value: Okta SSO header injection at CloudFront origin; all engineers with valid Okta session can read the portal. No write access for any user.
access_model.rationale: Read-only documentation portal requires only authentication, not authorization granularity

feature_scope.value: Service catalogue browseable by domain (payments, lending, identity, cards), interactive OpenAPI specs via Swagger UI embedded in static pages, SLA tier badges per service, squad ownership directory, changelog entries per service version, client stub download links
feature_scope.rationale: These features replace the current patchwork of Confluence pages and Google Docs that squads maintain manually

mvp_scope.value: Service catalogue with domain browsing, OpenAPI spec viewer for top 10 services, squad ownership display, automated CI publishing on spec file changes
mvp_scope.rationale: Enough to migrate the highest-traffic documentation away from manually maintained Confluence

security_baseline.value: Okta SSO via CloudFront Lambda@Edge header injection; HTTPS enforced; no write endpoints; S3 bucket policy blocks all public access; static content only
security_baseline.rationale: Internal portal with no sensitive data write path needs only authentication enforcement at CDN layer

frontend_stack.value: Astro 4 with TypeScript for static site generation; Swagger UI embedded components for OpenAPI spec rendering
frontend_stack.rationale: Astro generates zero-JS pages by default for fast load; Swagger UI provides interactive API spec exploration without server-side rendering

backend_stack.value: None — static site with no server-side runtime
backend_stack.rationale: All content generated at build time; no runtime backend required

data_platform.value: None — all data stored as YAML and JSON files in the monorepo; no database
data_platform.rationale: Service catalogue entries are version-controlled YAML files updated via PR; no database needed

hosting_target.value: S3 static hosting + CloudFront CDN with Lambda@Edge for Okta SSO header injection
hosting_target.rationale: S3 + CloudFront is the standard AWS pattern for internal static sites with zero operational overhead

privacy_retention_policy.value: No user data stored. CloudFront access logs retained 30 days for security investigation. No analytics PII collected.
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Read-only documentation portal with no user-generated content requires minimal retention policy

future_scope.value: Search functionality via pre-built Pagefind index, Datadog RUM for real user monitoring, automated client stub generation from OpenAPI specs in CI
future_scope.rationale: These features enhance developer experience without requiring a server-side runtime

constraints.value: Must build and publish within 5 minutes of a spec file merge, zero per-request cost model, no server-side runtime permitted
constraints.rationale: Fast publish cycle ensures documentation is always current; cost model must scale to thousands of page views without per-request charges

observability_baseline.value: AWS CloudWatch for CloudFront error rates and cache hit ratio; no application-level monitoring required for static content delivery
observability_baseline.rationale: Static site delivery is observable at CDN level without application instrumentation

execution_preference.value: Platform team builds and maintains CI publishing pipeline; squad teams update service catalogue YAML via standard PR workflow
execution_preference.rationale: Self-service model where squads own their service catalogue entries via PR reduces platform team burden

project.goal: Static developer portal for internal banking microservices API catalogue
project.target_users: Engineers across product squads

frontend.components: Swagger UI for OpenAPI spec rendering, domain browse pages, changelog views

security.auth: Okta SSO via CloudFront Lambda@Edge header injection

data.source: YAML and JSON files in monorepo

devops.pipeline: Astro build on spec file change, deploy to S3

reasoner_reviews.product: The service catalogue browsing by domain plus interactive OpenAPI spec viewer covers the core developer workflow. The CI publishing pipeline triggered on spec file merge is essential — engineers will stop trusting the portal if it goes stale. Changelog entries should be auto-generated from git history on the spec files rather than manually authored.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Astro 4 is the right SSG choice: it generates static HTML with zero JS overhead for browse pages, and Swagger UI components only hydrate on the interactive spec viewer pages. Pagefind pre-built search index is the correct zero-runtime search approach for future scope.
reasoner_reviews.security: Lambda@Edge for Okta header injection is a proven pattern for internal static sites. Ensure the Lambda function validates the Okta session token signature rather than trusting the header value alone. S3 bucket must have Block Public Access enabled and a bucket policy that only allows CloudFront OAC requests.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 5-minute build time is achievable for the current catalogue size. Monitor Astro build times as the number of OpenAPI spec pages grows beyond 100 services — parallel page generation may be needed.

frontend.app_structure: Astro 4 project with content collections for service_catalogue_entries (YAML), api_spec_versions (JSON OpenAPI files), squad_ownership_records (YAML), sla_tiers (YAML), changelog_entries (Markdown). Static pages generated at build time for each service entry.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Home page: domain navigation grid (payments, lending, identity, cards). Domain page: list of services in domain with SLA tier badges and squad ownership. Service page: service description, SLA tier, squad contact, OpenAPI spec via Swagger UI island component, changelog timeline. Squad directory page: list of squads and their owned services.
frontend.performance_notes: Astro island architecture: Swagger UI rendered as a client-side island only on service detail pages. Browse and directory pages are zero-JS static HTML. Astro prefetch on domain and service links for fast navigation. CloudFront cache headers: max-age=300 for HTML pages, max-age=86400 for static assets.

security.auth_design: Lambda@Edge function intercepts every CloudFront request, validates Okta session cookie via Okta introspection endpoint, returns 302 redirect to Okta login for unauthenticated requests. Authenticated requests pass through to S3 origin.
security.authorization_model: All authenticated engineers have read access to the full portal. No role-based content gating. Lambda@Edge enforces authentication only.
security.security_risks: Lambda@Edge cold start latency can add 100-500ms to first request after function inactivity. Mitigate by keeping function warm via scheduled CloudFront requests.

data.entities: service_catalogue_entries (service_name, domain ENUM payments/lending/identity/cards, description, owner_squad, sla_tier ENUM tier1/tier2/tier3, repo_url, api_spec_file_path, deprecated BOOLEAN). api_spec_versions (service_name, version, openapi_json_path, released_at, breaking_change BOOLEAN). squad_ownership_records (squad_name, slack_channel, team_lead, owned_services LIST). sla_tiers (tier_name, availability_target, response_time_p99, support_hours). changelog_entries (service_name, version, change_type ENUM added/changed/deprecated/removed, description, date).
data.storage_design: All data stored as YAML/JSON files in monorepo under /catalogue/ directory. Astro content collections read files at build time. No runtime database.
data.migration_notes: Initial population: platform team migrates existing Confluence service pages to YAML format. Squads assigned ownership of their service YAML files going forward.

devops.deployment_topology: S3 bucket (private, OAC-only access) serving Astro build output. CloudFront distribution with Lambda@Edge for Okta SSO, HTTPS redirect, and security headers. Route 53 CNAME: developer-portal.internal.bank.example.com.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions workflow triggered on changes to /catalogue/ or /src/ directories. Steps: npm install, astro build (generates /dist/), aws s3 sync dist/ to S3 bucket with --delete flag, CloudFront create-invalidation for changed paths.
devops.observability_stack: CloudWatch metrics for CloudFront: 4xxErrorRate, 5xxErrorRate, CacheHitRate. CloudWatch alarm on 5xxErrorRate > 1% for 5 minutes. No application-level APM needed for static delivery.

target_output.thinking_summary: This is a simple static_website — Astro 4 with S3 + CloudFront is the correct architecture. The intentional flaw in this plan is in the deployment_and_operations section, which incorrectly describes provisioning an ECS Fargate API service for dynamic catalogue search and a DynamoDB table for analytics event storage — both of which violate the static_website project class constraint that prohibits server-side runtime and databases.
target_output.title: Internal Banking Microservices API Developer Portal
target_output.executive_summary: A static developer portal built with Astro 4 and hosted on S3 + CloudFront that documents all internal banking microservices for engineers across product squads. Service catalogue entries, OpenAPI specs, squad ownership records, SLA tiers, and changelog entries are stored as YAML and JSON files in the monorepo and rendered into static pages at build time. A GitHub Actions pipeline rebuilds and publishes the portal within 5 minutes of any spec file merge. Authentication is enforced by Okta SSO via Lambda@Edge at CloudFront.
target_output.architecture_overview: Pure static site architecture: Astro 4 reads YAML service catalogue files and OpenAPI JSON specs from monorepo content collections at build time and generates static HTML pages. The resulting build output is deployed to a private S3 bucket. CloudFront distributes the static assets globally across internal AWS regions with Lambda@Edge enforcing Okta SSO on every request. No server-side runtime exists at request time — all pages are pre-generated. CloudFront cache headers differentiate HTML pages (5-minute TTL) from static assets like CSS and JavaScript (24-hour TTL). The Swagger UI OpenAPI explorer is rendered as an Astro island component that hydrates client-side only on service detail pages, keeping browse pages zero-JavaScript.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Astro 4 with TypeScript, Swagger UI 5.x as island component for OpenAPI exploration. Content: Astro content collections reading YAML (service catalogue, squad ownership, SLA tiers) and JSON (OpenAPI specs) from monorepo. Hosting: S3 static hosting (private bucket, OAC), CloudFront CDN with Lambda@Edge (Okta SSO). CI/CD: GitHub Actions (Astro build, S3 sync, CloudFront invalidation). Observability: AWS CloudWatch for CDN metrics.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Domain navigation: home page grid linking to payments, lending, identity, and cards domain sections. Service browse: domain pages listing all services with SLA tier badge (Tier 1/2/3) and squad ownership label. Service detail: full service description, squad contact with Slack channel, SLA commitment, OpenAPI interactive explorer via Swagger UI island, changelog timeline. Squad directory: list of all product squads with owned services and team lead contact. Spec search (future): Pagefind pre-built index for full-text search across all service descriptions and API paths.
target_output.system_components: S3 bucket (private, Block Public Access enabled, OAC-only policy, hosts Astro build output). CloudFront distribution (HTTPS-only, OAC origin, Lambda@Edge for Okta SSO, security headers). Lambda@Edge function (Okta session validation, unauthenticated redirect to Okta login). Route 53 CNAME (developer-portal.internal.bank.example.com). GitHub Actions runner (Astro build execution, S3 sync, CloudFront invalidation). Monorepo /catalogue/ directory (YAML and JSON source files for all content collections).
target_output.workflows: Spec Update Publish: Squad engineer merges PR updating their service OpenAPI spec JSON file in /catalogue/services/payments/charge-api/v2.json. GitHub Actions detects change to /catalogue/ directory, runs astro build, syncs /dist/ to S3 with --delete, creates CloudFront invalidation for /services/payments/charge-api/*. Portal reflects updated spec within 5 minutes of merge. Portal Browse: Engineer navigates to developer-portal.internal.bank.example.com. CloudFront Lambda@Edge validates Okta cookie; if absent, redirects to Okta login with return URL. On successful auth, CloudFront serves static home page from S3. Engineer clicks payments domain, browses to charge-api service detail page, expands OpenAPI spec section where Swagger UI island hydrates to show interactive endpoint explorer. Engineer downloads Python client stub from the stub download link.
