target_output.data_model: service_catalogue_entries YAML (service_name, domain ENUM payments/lending/identity/cards, description, owner_squad, sla_tier ENUM tier1/tier2/tier3, repo_url, api_spec_file_path, deprecated BOOLEAN). api_spec_versions JSON (service_name, version, openapi_json_path, released_at, breaking_change BOOLEAN). squad_ownership_records YAML (squad_name, slack_channel, team_lead, owned_services LIST). sla_tiers YAML (tier_name, availability_target STRING, p99_response_time STRING, support_hours STRING). changelog_entries Markdown frontmatter (service_name, version, change_type ENUM added/changed/deprecated/removed, description, date). All files version-controlled in monorepo; no runtime database.
target_output.api_design: No runtime HTTP API. Build-time data access only: Astro content collection getCollection('services') reads all service_catalogue_entries YAML files. getEntry('services', slug) fetches individual service entry. OpenAPI spec JSON files loaded via import() in Astro component to pass to Swagger UI island. No server endpoints, no REST API, no GraphQL. The portal is entirely read-only with no write surface.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Okta SSO enforced by Lambda@Edge on all CloudFront requests; unauthenticated requests receive 302 redirect to Okta login. Lambda@Edge validates Okta session token via introspection API before passing request to S3 origin. S3 bucket policy restricts access to CloudFront OAC only — no public access, no direct S3 URL access. CloudFront enforces HTTPS redirect for all HTTP requests. Security headers set by Lambda@Edge: Strict-Transport-Security, X-Frame-Options: DENY, Content-Security-Policy restricting script sources to self and cdn.jsdelivr.net for Swagger UI. CloudFront access logs retained 30 days in a separate S3 logging bucket.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Intentionally flawed section: In addition to S3 + CloudFront static hosting, this plan proposes provisioning an ECS Fargate microservice to handle real-time search queries against the service catalogue, backed by a DynamoDB table storing indexed service metadata and a Redis ElastiCache cluster for search result caching. The Fargate service would expose a POST /api/search endpoint integrated with the static site via a JavaScript fetch call. This runtime backend violates the static_website project class constraint and the contract field backend_stack which specifies no server-side runtime. This is an intentional flaw demonstrating a plan that contradicts its own contract. The correct approach is Pagefind pre-built search index embedded in the Astro build output.
target_output.observability: CloudWatch metrics for CloudFront distribution: 4xxErrorRate (alarm at >2% for 5 minutes), 5xxErrorRate (alarm at >0.5%), CacheHitRate (dashboard panel, target >90%). CloudFront access logs to S3 for security investigation. Lambda@Edge invocation errors tracked in CloudWatch Lambda metrics. No application-level APM needed — static content delivery requires only CDN-level observability.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Near-zero operational cost. S3 storage for Astro build output: <1GB estimated at $0.023/GB/month. CloudFront distribution: first 10TB/month included in AWS free tier for internal traffic volumes. Lambda@Edge invocations: ~$0.0006 per 1000 requests. Total estimated cost: under $20/month for internal portal traffic. Scales to any number of page views without per-request pricing concern beyond Lambda@Edge invocation cost.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (3 weeks): Astro 4 project setup with content collections for service catalogue YAML and OpenAPI JSON; domain and service browse pages; Swagger UI island component; squad ownership directory; Lambda@Edge Okta SSO; S3 + CloudFront hosting; GitHub Actions CI pipeline; initial catalogue population for top 10 services from payments and lending squads. Phase 2 (2 weeks): SLA tier badges, changelog timeline pages, client stub download links, squad directory, CloudWatch observability dashboard. Phase 3 (future): Pagefind pre-built search index, Datadog RUM for page view analytics, automated client stub generation in CI.
target_output.development_guidelines: All service catalogue YAML files validated against a JSON Schema in CI before Astro build runs — malformed entries fail the pipeline. TypeScript strict mode in Astro components. Swagger UI island components use client:visible directive to defer hydration until visible in viewport. CloudFront cache invalidation scoped to changed paths only (not wildcard /*) to minimize invalidation cost. Squads own their service YAML and OpenAPI spec files; PRs to /catalogue/ require approval from the platform team and the owning squad.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Build time grows beyond 5-minute SLA as service catalogue scales to 200+ services with large OpenAPI specs. Mitigation: Astro parallel page generation and incremental builds in future version; monitor build times in CI and alert when approaching 4 minutes. Risk: Lambda@Edge cold start adds latency for first request after function inactivity. Mitigation: scheduled CloudFront requests to keep function warm. Tradeoff: Pre-built static pages over server-side rendering eliminates runtime costs but means search requires a pre-built Pagefind index rebuilt with every deploy rather than real-time indexing.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed Astro over Next.js static export for zero-JS browse pages and simpler build pipeline. Confirmed YAML files in monorepo over a CMS for service catalogue to keep squad ownership via PR workflow. Confirmed Lambda@Edge over Cognito Authorizer because Cognito requires API Gateway, which introduces a server-side runtime.

metadata.notes: Intentionally flawed: deployment_and_operations describes ECS Fargate search service and DynamoDB table violating static_website project class constraint. All other sections are correct.

profile.domain: Domain-Driven Microservices Decomposition Research Prototype for Mortgage Origination

project_goal.value: Research prototype exploring domain-driven decomposition of a monolithic mortgage origination system into bounded-context microservices, defining aggregate boundaries for application intake, underwriting, and closing workflows
project_goal.rationale: The existing mortgage origination monolith has become a delivery bottleneck; this prototype validates decomposition boundaries before committing to a full migration program

target_users.value: Enterprise architects and senior engineers at the bank evaluating decomposition strategies for the mortgage origination platform
target_users.rationale: Research output will be used by the architecture review board to decide on decomposition approach

project_class.rationale: Exploratory prototype to validate DDD boundary design, not a production system; results feed into migration planning, not live operations

capabilities.rationale: Prototype includes a UI for bounded-context canvas design, backend scaffold generation, data model analysis, and integration test analytics reporting

complexity_level.rationale: DDD aggregate boundary analysis, event contract generation, and replay of historical loan transaction datasets against skeleton services require significant technical depth

risk_level.rationale: Handles sample loan origination data that must be masked under SOX; results feed sensitive architectural decisions but prototype itself is internal-only research

data_sensitivity.rationale: Sample loan data used in integration tests contains masked but structurally realistic borrower financials and mortgage terms

external_exposure.rationale: Prototype accessible only to architecture team on internal Azure tenant behind Azure AD authentication

access_model.value: Azure AD authentication; role-based access with Architect (full access to canvas design and scaffold generation) and Reviewer (read-only access to published decomposition reports)
access_model.rationale: Architects design the bounded contexts; reviewers on the architecture board need read access to evaluate proposals

feature_scope.value: Bounded-context canvas editor for defining aggregate boundaries and domain events, skeleton microservice scaffold generator from canvas definitions, integration test harness replaying historical loan transactions against decomposed services, parity gap analytics comparing decomposed service behavior against monolith baseline, dependency graph visualization
feature_scope.rationale: These features collectively validate whether a proposed decomposition boundary set is viable for the mortgage origination domain

mvp_scope.value: Bounded-context canvas editor, skeleton service scaffold generator for application-intake and underwriting contexts, integration test harness with historical loan transaction replay
mvp_scope.rationale: Canvas editor plus scaffold generation plus replay harness are the minimum to produce evaluable results for the architecture board

security_baseline.value: Azure AD SSO for all access, sample loan data masked via SOX data masking controls before use in prototype, AKS pod-level network policies restricting cross-context communication during integration tests, all prototype data in Azure-encrypted storage
security_baseline.rationale: Confidential data sensitivity and SOX requirement demand masking and access controls even in a research context

frontend_stack.value: Next.js 14 with TypeScript for bounded-context canvas editor and analytics dashboard
frontend_stack.rationale: Next.js 14 App Router supports complex interactive canvas UI and server-side rendering for analytics pages

backend_stack.value: Java 21 with Spring Boot 3 for scaffold generator service and integration test orchestrator
backend_stack.rationale: Java 21 virtual threads suit the concurrent integration test execution; Spring Boot 3 aligns with the bank existing Java expertise

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for bounded context canvas definitions and integration test results; Redis 7 for test run state
data_platform.rationale: Relational schema suits the structured canvas entity relationships; Redis tracks live test run progress for real-time dashboard updates

hosting_target.value: AKS (Azure Kubernetes Service) on internal Azure tenant
hosting_target.rationale: AKS provides the Kubernetes environment needed for skeleton service deployment during integration tests

privacy_retention_policy.value: Sample loan data masked before use; masked dataset retained for prototype duration only (max 12 months); integration test results retained 12 months for architecture board reference; no unmasked loan data in any prototype environment
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: SOX requires data masking and retention controls even for internal architectural research using structurally realistic data

future_scope.value: Automated bounded-context sizing recommendations from coupling analysis, event storming session export to canvas format, integration with migration planning tooling for wave sequencing
future_scope.rationale: Downstream tools will consume prototype output once decomposition boundaries are validated

constraints.value: Prototype budget capped at $800/month Azure spend; integration test replay must complete within 30 minutes for a full historical transaction dataset; research findings must be presentable to architecture board by Q3
constraints.rationale: Budget constraint keeps prototype lightweight; 30-minute test run fits within an architecture team working session

observability_baseline.value: Azure Monitor + Application Insights for Spring Boot services; integration test run progress streamed to Next.js dashboard via Redis pub/sub
observability_baseline.rationale: Lightweight observability for a prototype; Application Insights integrates natively with AKS

execution_preference.value: Architecture team iterates in 2-week research sprints; findings documented after each sprint; architecture board review at end of each sprint
execution_preference.rationale: Research prototype benefits from short review cycles to course-correct decomposition hypotheses early

compliance_context.value: SOX Section 302 and 404 data integrity controls; sample loan data must be masked using Faker-generated synthetic borrower profiles replacing real names, SSNs, and account numbers before any use in the prototype environment
compliance_context.rationale: Data sensitivity is confidential due to structurally realistic masked loan data that maps to real mortgage product terms

project.goal: Research prototype validating DDD bounded-context boundaries for mortgage origination decomposition
project.target_users: Enterprise architects and senior engineers

frontend.features: Canvas editor for bounded-context design, analytics dashboard for parity gap reporting, dependency graph visualization

backend.framework: Spring Boot 3
backend.modules: Canvas persistence service, scaffold generator, integration test orchestrator

security.auth: Azure AD SSO
security.data_masking: SOX-compliant masking of sample loan data

data.schema: bounded_contexts, aggregates, domain_events, service_contracts, integration_tests, dependency_graphs

reasoner_reviews.product: The canvas editor is the most critical feature — architects need to define bounded contexts interactively and see immediate scaffold generation output. Parity gap analytics are the primary deliverable for the architecture board. Ensure the gap report clearly shows which loan transaction types succeed and fail against each decomposed service boundary, not just aggregate pass/fail counts.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Spring Boot 3 with Java 21 virtual threads is appropriate for the concurrent integration test orchestrator that runs dozens of skeleton services simultaneously during transaction replay. The scaffold generator should produce real compilable Spring Boot project stubs, not just skeleton files, so the architecture team can immediately deploy them to AKS for testing.
reasoner_reviews.security: SOX data masking must be applied by a dedicated masking pipeline before the loan transaction dataset enters the prototype environment — the scaffold generator and test harness must never have access to the unmasked production dataset. AKS NetworkPolicy rules must isolate bounded-context services during integration tests to validate that inter-context communication only occurs through published domain event contracts.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 30-minute test run budget for full transaction replay constrains the integration test harness design. Parallel test execution across bounded-context service instances on AKS is required; sequential replay would exceed the time budget. Monitor AKS resource consumption during test runs to stay within the $800/month budget.

backend.service_design: Three Spring Boot 3 services: CanvasService (CRUD for bounded_contexts, aggregates, domain_events, service_contracts in PostgreSQL), ScaffoldGeneratorService (generates Spring Boot 3 project stubs from canvas definitions, zips and stores in Azure Blob Storage), IntegrationTestOrchestrator (deploys skeleton services to AKS test namespace, replays masked loan transactions, collects parity gap metrics).
backend.business_modules: BoundedContextAnalyzer: computes coupling scores between bounded contexts from the dependency graph to surface overly coupled boundary proposals. EventContractGenerator: produces AsyncAPI 2.6 event contract YAML from domain_events definitions in the canvas. TransactionReplayEngine: reads masked loan transaction dataset from Azure Blob, routes each transaction to the appropriate bounded-context skeleton service, captures response and compares to monolith baseline output.
backend.scaling_notes: Integration test runs require parallel skeleton service deployment on AKS. Use a dedicated test namespace per run with resource quotas. Java 21 virtual threads allow the orchestrator to manage hundreds of concurrent transaction replays without thread pool exhaustion.
backend.backend_risks: Skeleton service scaffolds are intentionally minimal and will return stub responses, not real business logic. Parity comparison must account for stub vs real behavior gap — the test harness should only evaluate request routing correctness (does the right bounded-context service receive the right transaction type?) not business logic correctness.

frontend.app_structure: Next.js 14 App Router with three main route groups: /canvas (bounded-context editor), /analytics (parity gap dashboard and dependency graph), /reports (architecture board presentation exports).
frontend.pages_and_flows: Canvas editor: drag-and-drop bounded-context definition with aggregate and domain event assignment. Real-time scaffold generation preview showing which Spring Boot modules will be generated. Scaffold download button. Parity gap dashboard: transaction replay results table filterable by loan product type (purchase, refinance, HELOC), parity gap % per bounded context, drill-down into individual failing transaction types. Dependency graph visualization: force-directed graph of bounded-context coupling scores.
frontend.state_management: React Query for canvas data (bounded_contexts, aggregates, domain_events) with optimistic updates. Redis pub/sub via Server-Sent Events for live test run progress streaming to parity gap dashboard.

security.auth_design: Azure AD SSO via NextAuth.js. MSAL token injected into Spring Boot API calls from Next.js server components. AKS workload identity for Spring Boot service accounts.
security.authorization_model: Architect role (Azure AD group): full CRUD on canvas definitions, can trigger scaffold generation and integration test runs. Reviewer role: read-only on canvas definitions and parity gap reports, cannot trigger test runs.
security.privacy_controls: Masked loan transaction dataset stored in a dedicated Azure Blob Storage container with access restricted to the IntegrationTestOrchestrator service account. Data masking audit trail logged to a separate Azure Storage Table.

data.entities: bounded_contexts (id UUID PK, name, domain ENUM application_intake/underwriting/closing, description, owner_squad, created_by, created_at). aggregates (id UUID PK, context_id FK, name, root_entity, invariants TEXT). domain_events (id UUID PK, context_id FK, event_name, payload_schema JSONB, source_aggregate, target_contexts JSONB). service_contracts (id UUID PK, context_id FK, contract_type ENUM sync/async, spec_format ENUM openapi/asyncapi, spec_content TEXT). integration_tests (id UUID PK, run_id UUID, context_id FK, transaction_type, outcome ENUM pass/fail/stub_response, parity_gap_details JSONB, executed_at). dependency_graphs (id UUID PK, canvas_version_id FK, coupling_matrix JSONB, generated_at).
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server for canvas entities. Redis 7 on Azure Cache for Redis for live test run progress pub/sub. Azure Blob Storage for masked loan transaction datasets and generated scaffold ZIP files.
data.retention_and_deletion: Canvas definitions retained for prototype duration (12 months max). Integration test results retained 12 months for architecture board reference. Masked loan dataset deleted when prototype is decommissioned.

devops.deployment_topology: AKS cluster with two namespaces: prototype-app (CanvasService, ScaffoldGeneratorService, IntegrationTestOrchestrator, Next.js frontend) and prototype-test (ephemeral skeleton service deployments per test run, auto-deleted after run completion). Azure DevOps Pipelines for CI/CD.
devops.ci_cd_design: Azure DevOps Pipeline: Maven build + JUnit tests for Spring Boot services, ESLint + Jest for Next.js, Docker build, push to Azure Container Registry, Helm chart deploy to prototype-app namespace. Test runs deploy skeleton services to prototype-test namespace via IntegrationTestOrchestrator Kubernetes API calls.
devops.observability_stack: Application Insights SDK in Spring Boot services for request tracing and exception logging. Azure Monitor dashboards for AKS pod CPU/memory during test runs. Next.js instrumentation via Application Insights JavaScript SDK.
devops.devops_risks: prototype-test namespace resource consumption during test runs can spike unexpectedly if many skeleton services are deployed simultaneously. Set resource quotas at namespace level and alert on quota utilization above 80%.

ISS-001.title: Integration test harness parity comparison logic was too coarse — only aggregate pass/fail per bounded context, not per transaction type
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 parity gap analytics only reported a single pass/fail percentage per bounded context. Architecture board reviewers need drill-down by loan product type (purchase, refinance, HELOC) and by individual failing transaction type to make decomposition decisions.

history.detail: Parity gap dashboard showed only aggregate pass/fail counts
Added transaction-type-level drill-down and loan product type filter to parity gap dashboard

ISS-002.title: Scaffold generator produced skeleton files only, not compilable Spring Boot project stubs
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 scaffold generator output was a directory of empty Java class files. The architecture team needs compilable Spring Boot stubs they can immediately deploy to AKS test namespace without manual scaffolding work.

history.detail: Scaffold output was non-compilable empty class files
Scaffold generator now produces Maven-structured Spring Boot 3 projects with main class, controller stubs, and pom.xml

ISS-003.title: SOX data masking controls not described in security_and_compliance section
ISS-003.detail: Rounds 1 and 2 security sections mentioned masking but did not specify the masking pipeline implementation, what fields are masked, or how the masking audit trail is maintained as required by SOX Section 302.

history.detail: Masking mentioned in one sentence with no technical detail
Still no masking pipeline description or field-level masking specification

focus_issues.title: SOX data masking controls not described in security_and_compliance section
focus_issues.detail: Two prior rounds have not adequately described the SOX masking pipeline, masked fields, or audit trail mechanism.

latest_recommendations: Add specific SOX data masking pipeline description to security_and_compliance: which fields are masked, what masking technique is used (Faker substitution vs tokenization), where the masking pipeline runs, and how the masking audit trail is written to Azure Storage Table
revision_memory.latest_plan_title: Mortgage Origination DDD Decomposition Prototype — Round 2

convergence_state.reason: ISS-003 medium severity unresolved after 2 rounds

previous_audits.summary: Strong improvement in round 2 resolving the parity gap granularity and scaffold generator issues. Remaining gap is the underspecified SOX masking pipeline in security_and_compliance which is required given data_sensitivity=confidential.
strengths: Parity gap dashboard now provides transaction-type drill-down adequate for architecture board decision-making
Scaffold generator produces compilable Spring Boot 3 Maven projects deployable to AKS test namespace
concerns: security_and_compliance section still describes masking as a single sentence without pipeline details, field list, or audit trail mechanism
blocking_issues: SOX masking pipeline must be specified with field-level detail and audit trail mechanism given compliance_context requirement
recommendations: Describe the masking pipeline as a pre-processing step: which fields (borrower SSN, name, account numbers, phone) are replaced with Faker-generated synthetic values, where the pipeline runs (Azure Data Factory pipeline or one-time Python script), and how audit records (original_field_hash, masked_value, timestamp) are written to Azure Storage Table

issue_updates.title: SOX data masking controls underspecified
issue_updates.detail: Security section still only says 'data is masked' without pipeline implementation or audit trail details

previous_plan.title: Mortgage Origination DDD Decomposition Prototype — Round 2
previous_plan.executive_summary: Research prototype enabling architects to define DDD bounded contexts for mortgage origination, generate Spring Boot 3 scaffold services, and replay masked loan transactions to measure parity gaps.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Next.js 14 canvas editor communicating with Spring Boot 3 services on AKS. Three backend services: CanvasService, ScaffoldGeneratorService, IntegrationTestOrchestrator. PostgreSQL for canvas data, Redis for test run streaming.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: Azure AD SSO for all access. Sample loan data masked before use in prototype. AKS NetworkPolicy for cross-context isolation.
previous_plan.workflows: Architects design bounded contexts in canvas editor. Scaffold generator produces compilable Spring Boot Maven stubs from canvas definitions. Integration test orchestrator replays masked loan transactions and reports parity gaps.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 3 needs to resolve ISS-003 by adding specific SOX masking pipeline detail to security_and_compliance. ISS-001 and ISS-002 were resolved in round 2. The plan is otherwise sound — Spring Boot 3 on AKS for the scaffold generator and test orchestrator, Next.js 14 canvas editor, PostgreSQL + Redis for data. The fix report must address the masking pipeline gap with field-level specification and audit trail mechanism.

fix_report.action_taken: Added detailed SOX data masking pipeline to security_and_compliance: specified masked fields (borrower SSN, full name, account numbers, phone, employer name), masking technique (Faker-generated substitution for PII fields, SHA-256 HMAC tokenization for account numbers to preserve referential integrity across transaction records), pipeline execution (Azure Data Factory pipeline run once before prototype onboarding, producing a masked dataset ZIP in Azure Blob Storage), and audit trail mechanism (DataMaskingAuditRecord written to Azure Storage Table with original_field_hash, masked_value_hash, field_name, run_id, timestamp for SOX Section 302 attestation)
fix_report.expected_outcome: security_and_compliance section now satisfies SOX Section 302 data masking documentation requirement with field-level specification and audit trail

target_output.title: Mortgage Origination DDD Decomposition Prototype Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A research prototype enabling enterprise architects to model DDD bounded-context boundaries for the mortgage origination monolith decomposition and validate their viability through automated integration testing. Architects design application-intake, underwriting, and closing bounded contexts in an interactive canvas editor (Next.js 14), generate compilable Spring Boot 3 skeleton services from canvas definitions, and replay masked historical loan transactions through the deployed skeleton services to measure parity gaps against the monolith baseline. Results feed the architecture board's decomposition decision for the full migration program.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three-tier prototype on AKS with two Kubernetes namespaces. In prototype-app namespace: Next.js 14 App Router frontend (canvas editor, analytics dashboard), three Spring Boot 3 backend services (CanvasService, ScaffoldGeneratorService, IntegrationTestOrchestrator), PostgreSQL 16 for canvas entity persistence, and Redis 7 for test run progress pub/sub. In prototype-test namespace: ephemeral skeleton microservice deployments per integration test run, auto-deleted after run completion. Azure AD SSO enforced via NextAuth.js on the frontend with MSAL token propagation to backend services. Masked loan transaction datasets stored in Azure Blob Storage, accessible only to IntegrationTestOrchestrator service account. Integration test runs complete within 30 minutes via parallel skeleton service deployment and Java 21 virtual thread concurrency in the orchestrator.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Next.js 14 with TypeScript, App Router, React Query, NextAuth.js (Azure AD), Server-Sent Events for test run streaming. Backend: Java 21 with Spring Boot 3, Maven, Spring Data JPA, Spring Security with MSAL. Database: PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server. Cache: Redis 7 on Azure Cache for Redis. Storage: Azure Blob Storage for masked datasets and scaffold ZIPs. CI/CD: Azure DevOps Pipelines, Helm charts. Observability: Application Insights SDK. Infrastructure: AKS (Azure Kubernetes Service), Azure Container Registry.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Canvas editor: drag-and-drop bounded-context definition with aggregate and domain event assignment, real-time scaffold generation preview, scaffold ZIP download. Scaffold generator: produces Maven-structured Spring Boot 3 projects with main class, REST controller stubs, and pom.xml per bounded context. Integration test orchestrator: deploys skeleton services to prototype-test namespace, routes masked loan transactions by type, captures responses, compares to monolith baseline outputs. Parity gap dashboard: transaction replay results filterable by loan product type (purchase, refinance, HELOC), drill-down into failing transaction types per bounded context. Dependency graph: force-directed visualization of coupling scores between bounded contexts from BoundedContextAnalyzer.
target_output.system_components: prototype-app AKS namespace (CanvasService Spring Boot, ScaffoldGeneratorService Spring Boot, IntegrationTestOrchestrator Spring Boot, Next.js frontend deployment). prototype-test AKS namespace (ephemeral skeleton service pods per test run). Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server (canvas entities). Azure Cache for Redis (test run pub/sub). Azure Blob Storage (masked loan transaction dataset, scaffold ZIP archives). Azure Container Registry (service Docker images). Azure AD (identity provider, MSAL tokens). Application Insights (telemetry). Azure DevOps (CI/CD pipelines).
target_output.workflows: Bounded-Context Design: Architect opens canvas editor, drags application-intake bounded context onto canvas, adds LoanApplication aggregate with root entity and invariants, assigns LoanSubmitted and DocumentsUploaded domain events. Real-time preview shows what Spring Boot modules will be generated. Architect clicks Generate Scaffold — ScaffoldGeneratorService produces Maven project with LoanApplicationController stub, LoanApplication entity, and AsyncAPI event contract YAML, zips it, and stores in Azure Blob. Integration Test Run: Architect triggers test run from dashboard. IntegrationTestOrchestrator deploys scaffold services to prototype-test namespace with AKS NetworkPolicy isolating them. Masked loan transaction dataset is loaded from Azure Blob; each transaction is routed to the appropriate bounded-context service. Java 21 virtual threads handle concurrent transaction replay. Response outcomes are stored to integration_tests table. Redis pub/sub streams progress to Next.js dashboard via SSE. After completion, prototype-test namespace resources are deleted. Parity gap report is generated and available to Reviewer role.
target_output.data_model: bounded_contexts (id UUID PK, name VARCHAR, domain ENUM application_intake/underwriting/closing, description TEXT, owner_squad VARCHAR, created_by VARCHAR, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). aggregates (id UUID PK, context_id UUID FK, name VARCHAR, root_entity VARCHAR, invariants TEXT). domain_events (id UUID PK, context_id UUID FK, event_name VARCHAR, payload_schema JSONB, source_aggregate VARCHAR, target_contexts JSONB). service_contracts (id UUID PK, context_id UUID FK, contract_type ENUM sync/async, spec_format ENUM openapi/asyncapi, spec_content TEXT). integration_tests (id UUID PK, run_id UUID, context_id UUID FK, transaction_type VARCHAR, loan_product_type ENUM purchase/refinance/heloc, outcome ENUM pass/fail/stub_response, parity_gap_details JSONB, executed_at TIMESTAMPTZ). dependency_graphs (id UUID PK, canvas_version_id UUID FK, coupling_matrix JSONB, generated_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: REST API from Spring Boot CanvasService: GET /api/v1/contexts (list bounded contexts), POST /api/v1/contexts (create), GET /api/v1/contexts/{id}/aggregates, POST /api/v1/contexts/{id}/aggregates (add aggregate). ScaffoldGeneratorService: POST /api/v1/scaffolds (generate scaffold for context id, returns blob URL). IntegrationTestOrchestrator: POST /api/v1/test-runs (initiate replay run, returns run_id), GET /api/v1/test-runs/{run_id}/status (SSE endpoint for progress), GET /api/v1/test-runs/{run_id}/results (parity gap results). All endpoints require Azure AD Bearer token. Next.js calls these via server-component fetch with MSAL token from session.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Azure AD SSO via NextAuth.js; MSAL Bearer token required for all backend API calls. RBAC: Architect group (full CRUD, test run triggers), Reviewer group (read-only canvas and reports). AKS NetworkPolicy in prototype-test namespace: each bounded-context skeleton service pod blocked from calling other context pods — inter-context communication only via published event contract endpoints to validate isolation. SOX data masking pipeline: a one-time Azure Data Factory pipeline run before prototype onboarding masks the loan transaction dataset. Masked fields: borrower_full_name (Faker.person.fullName substitution), borrower_ssn (Faker.id substitution), employer_name (Faker.company substitution), phone_number (Faker.phone substitution), account_numbers (SHA-256 HMAC tokenization preserving referential integrity across transactions). Masking audit trail: DataMaskingAuditRecord written to Azure Storage Table per field per record — fields: original_field_hash (SHA-256 of original value), masked_value_hash, field_name, dataset_run_id, timestamp. Audit table retained 7 years for SOX Section 302 attestation. Masked dataset stored in dedicated Azure Blob container with Architect service account access only — IntegrationTestOrchestrator uses workload identity with read-only binding to this container.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Azure DevOps Pipeline: Maven build + JUnit tests (Spring Boot services, 85% coverage gate), ESLint + Jest (Next.js), Docker build per service, push to Azure Container Registry, Helm chart deploy to prototype-app namespace via kubectl apply. Prototype-test namespace resources managed entirely by IntegrationTestOrchestrator at runtime — AKS service account has create/delete permissions scoped to prototype-test namespace only. Infrastructure provisioned via Terraform: AKS cluster, Azure Database for PostgreSQL, Azure Cache for Redis, Azure Blob Storage accounts, Azure Container Registry, Application Insights workspace. Single research environment; no staging/production separation. Cost control: AKS spot node pool for prototype-test pods (up to 60% cost reduction vs on-demand). Monthly Azure spend monitored via Azure Cost Management alert at $700 threshold (below $800 budget cap).
target_output.observability: Application Insights SDK in Spring Boot services: request/response tracing, exception logging with stack traces, custom metrics for scaffold_generation_duration_ms and test_run_transaction_count. Application Insights JavaScript SDK in Next.js for page load performance and SSE connection tracking. Azure Monitor dashboards: integration test run duration (line chart over sprints), parity gap % by bounded context (bar chart), AKS prototype-test namespace pod count during test runs (to monitor resource usage). Alert: test run duration > 25 minutes (approaching 30-minute budget).
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Target under $800/month Azure spend. PostgreSQL Flexible Server (Burstable B2ms): ~$90/month. Azure Cache for Redis (C1 Basic): ~$55/month. AKS cluster (2 nodes D2s_v3 on-demand + spot node pool for test namespace): ~$400/month. Azure Blob Storage: ~$10/month. Application Insights ingestion: ~$20/month. Container Registry Basic: ~$5/month. Total estimated: ~$580/month with headroom. Spot nodes reduce test namespace cost by ~50% compared to on-demand.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 Sprint 1-2 (4 weeks): Canvas editor with bounded-context CRUD, aggregate and domain event assignment, PostgreSQL schema, Azure AD auth, AKS deployment, Azure DevOps pipeline. Board review: demo canvas editor. Phase 2 Sprint 3 (2 weeks): ScaffoldGeneratorService producing compilable Spring Boot Maven stubs, scaffold download. SOX masking pipeline (ADF one-time run). Board review: demo scaffold generation from canvas. Phase 3 Sprint 4-5 (4 weeks): IntegrationTestOrchestrator with transaction replay and parity gap analytics, Redis SSE streaming, parity gap dashboard with drill-down, dependency graph visualization. Board review: present parity gap findings and decomposition recommendation.
target_output.development_guidelines: Java 21 strict nullability annotations in Spring Boot services; no unchecked casts in scaffold code generator. Next.js server components for all data-fetching routes; client components only for canvas editor interactive elements. Azure AD token validation via Spring Security MSAL adapter — no manual JWT parsing. Integration test results stored atomically per run; partial run results not exposed on dashboard until run completes. Canvas definitions versioned via optimistic locking (version INTEGER on bounded_contexts) to prevent concurrent edit conflicts.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: 30-minute test run budget is tight for large loan transaction datasets — if the masked dataset grows beyond 50,000 transactions, parallel skeleton service throughput may be insufficient. Mitigation: IntegrationTestOrchestrator uses Java 21 virtual threads for concurrency; add configurable transaction sampling rate if run times approach 25 minutes. Risk: Skeleton service stubs return fixed synthetic responses, so parity gap comparison measures routing correctness only, not business logic correctness. This is acceptable for the prototype goal of validating aggregate boundaries. Tradeoff: Single AKS cluster for both prototype-app and prototype-test namespaces reduces cost but means a misbehaving test run (excessive pod creation) could starve prototype-app pods; mitigate with namespace-level ResourceQuota on prototype-test.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed SHA-256 HMAC tokenization for account numbers (not Faker substitution) to preserve referential integrity across loan transaction records — multiple records referencing the same account number must still match in the masked dataset. Confirmed Azure Data Factory over a Python script for masking pipeline to align with the bank's existing ADF governance controls. Confirmed Redis pub/sub over WebSockets for test run streaming due to simpler infrastructure requirements in the prototype context.

metadata.notes: Round 3 research prototype for mortgage origination DDD decomposition. Resolves ISS-003 SOX masking pipeline gap from rounds 1-2. ISS-001 and ISS-002 were resolved in round 2.

profile.domain: General Ledger Reconciliation Automation for Core Banking and Payment Networks

project_goal.value: Automate reconciliation of daily general ledger entries across core banking, card processing, and payment networks by ingesting batch files, detecting discrepancies, and routing exception items to the correct operations team queue
project_goal.rationale: Manual reconciliation of ISO 20022 payment files and SWIFT MT940 statements takes 6 hours daily and has a 2% error rate that creates downstream audit findings

target_users.value: Finance operations staff who work exception queues and sign off on reconciliation runs; internal audit who reviews reconciliation attestation reports
target_users.rationale: Operations staff need a queue UI to work exceptions; audit needs immutable reconciliation audit trails

project_class.rationale: Batch-driven backend automation tool with an exceptions queue workflow; no public-facing product

capabilities.rationale: Requires batch ingestion of payment files, integration with core banking and card processors, data reconciliation engine, and exception queue workflow

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-source file format ingestion (ISO 20022 XML, SWIFT MT940, CSV), matching rule engine, dual-approver exception sign-off workflow, and SOX audit trail requirements

risk_level.rationale: Directly handles financial general ledger entries and payment reconciliation data subject to SOX Section 404 financial controls

data_sensitivity.rationale: Processes and stores general ledger entries, transaction amounts, account numbers, and payment routing information

external_exposure.rationale: Accessible only to authenticated finance operations staff on internal network

access_model.value: Keycloak RBAC with roles: reconciliation-operator (work exception queue, view reconciliation runs), reconciliation-approver (sign-off on exceptions, requires dual-approver), reconciliation-admin (configure tolerance thresholds and matching rules), audit-viewer (read-only access to reconciliation runs and audit trail)
access_model.rationale: SOX Section 404 requires segregation of duties between operators who work exceptions and approvers who sign off

feature_scope.value: Nightly batch ingestion of ISO 20022 XML payment files and SWIFT MT940 statements, GL entry matching engine applying configurable tolerance thresholds, automatic balanced journal entry posting for matched items, exception item detection and routing to operations team queues by exception type, dual-approver exception sign-off workflow, correcting GL entry posting on exception resolution, reconciliation run audit trail for SOX attestation
feature_scope.rationale: These features automate the full reconciliation lifecycle from file ingestion to audit-ready close

mvp_scope.value: Nightly batch ingestion for ISO 20022 payment files, matching engine with static tolerance thresholds, exception item queue with single-approver sign-off, reconciliation run status dashboard for operations staff
mvp_scope.rationale: MVP automates the highest-volume reconciliation workflow (payment files) and enables operations staff to work exceptions in a queue instead of spreadsheets

security_baseline.value: Keycloak SSO with MFA for all roles, TLS 1.3 for all internal service communication, AES-256 encryption for GL entry data at rest, immutable audit log for all reconciliation actions, segregation of duties enforced by dual-approver workflow
security_baseline.rationale: SOX financial controls require strong authentication, encryption, and immutable audit trail for all GL reconciliation operations

frontend_stack.value: None — backend automation tool; operations staff access via a minimal internal web queue UI served by the backend (server-rendered HTML) or existing internal operations portal integration
frontend_stack.rationale: No dedicated frontend SPA; exception queue workflow is accessed via server-rendered pages on the same Flask service or via an existing internal operations portal API integration

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with Celery for task queue and batch orchestration
backend_stack.rationale: Celery provides mature task scheduling, retry handling, and distributed worker support for the nightly batch ingestion and exception routing workflows

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for reconciliation records, exception items, and audit trail; Redis 7 for Celery broker and result backend
data_platform.rationale: Relational schema suits the structured reconciliation entity relationships and supports complex matching queries; Redis is the standard Celery broker

hosting_target.value: ECS Fargate on AWS in us-east-1
hosting_target.rationale: Managed container runtime with no server provisioning overhead; data residency requirement satisfied by us-east-1 single-region deployment

privacy_retention_policy.value: Reconciliation records and GL entries retained 7 years per SOX and banking regulation. Audit trail records immutable and retained 7 years. Exception items retained 7 years after resolution. Source batch files archived to S3 Glacier after 90 days.
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: SOX Section 404 financial record retention requirements mandate 7-year retention for all reconciliation and GL data

future_scope.value: ML-based anomaly detection for unusual reconciliation discrepancy patterns, SWIFT MT950 statement support, integration with external auditor reporting portal, automated correcting entry posting to core banking API
future_scope.rationale: ML anomaly detection and automated correction posting are high-value features deferred from MVP to avoid scope creep

constraints.value: Reconciliation batch must complete by 06:00 UTC before trading day open; no reconciliation data may leave us-east-1 region; dual-approver sign-off required for exceptions above $10,000 threshold
constraints.rationale: 06:00 UTC deadline ensures reconciled positions are available to treasury before markets open; dual-approver threshold is an internal SOX control

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM for Celery worker task tracing, reconciliation run duration monitoring, exception queue depth alerting; PagerDuty escalation if batch fails to complete by 05:30 UTC
observability_baseline.rationale: Finance operations must be alerted to batch failures before the 06:00 trading deadline so manual fallback procedures can be initiated

execution_preference.value: Agile delivery in 2-week sprints; infrastructure as code via Terraform; all database migrations via Alembic with rollback scripts
execution_preference.rationale: Alembic migration rollback scripts are required given the sensitivity of GL data schema changes

compliance_context.value: SOX Section 404 financial controls: all GL reconciliation actions must be logged to immutable audit trail; dual-approver sign-off required for exception resolution above threshold; reconciliation attestation report generated per run for internal audit. Basel III operational risk controls: SLA tracking for batch completion time against trading day deadline. Internal audit trail requirements: every operator action on exception queue items logged with user identity, timestamp, and action type.
compliance_context.rationale: SOX and Basel III requirements apply given the system directly manages GL entries and payment reconciliation data

project.goal: Automate nightly GL reconciliation across core banking, card processing, and payment networks with exception queue workflow
project.target_users: Finance operations staff and internal audit
project.timeline: MVP by Q3; full feature set Q4

backend.framework: Celery task queue + Flask for queue UI

security.auth: Keycloak SSO with MFA
security.encryption: AES-256 at rest, TLS 1.3 in transit

data.schema: ledger_entries, reconciliation_runs, exception_items, source_file_manifests, operations_queues, tolerance_thresholds

reasoner_reviews.product: The matching engine tolerance threshold configuration is critical to operations adoption — if thresholds are too tight, operators are overwhelmed with false-positive exceptions; too loose, real discrepancies are auto-matched incorrectly. The tolerance_thresholds table should support per-source-system and per-transaction-type configuration, not a single global tolerance. Exception routing by exception type (timing difference, amount mismatch, missing counterparty) reduces operator cognitive load.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Celery Beat is the right scheduler for the nightly batch trigger at 02:00 UTC. The MatchingEngine should process ledger entries in memory sorted by transaction reference, not via SQL self-joins, to meet the 06:00 UTC completion deadline for large volumes. Consider partitioning the ledger_entries table by reconciliation_date to keep query performance stable as historical data accumulates.
reasoner_reviews.security: Dual-approver sign-off must be enforced at the application layer with database-level constraint: an exception_items record cannot transition to closed status unless two distinct approver_ids are recorded in the approval_actions table. Keycloak JWT must include the user role claim validated on every exception queue action. The audit_log table must be append-only with a database-level trigger preventing UPDATE and DELETE.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 06:00 UTC batch completion deadline requires monitoring at multiple checkpoints: file arrival (02:00), ingestion complete (03:00), matching complete (04:30), exception routing complete (05:00). PagerDuty alert at 05:30 UTC if any checkpoint is missed. Ensure Celery worker count scales for peak file volumes (end-of-month batch can be 3x normal daily volume).

backend.service_design: Three Celery applications sharing a PostgreSQL and Redis instance: ReconciliationBatch (Beat scheduler + workers for nightly ingestion, matching, and exception routing), ExceptionQueueAPI (Flask app serving exception queue UI and approval workflow endpoints), AuditService (append-only audit log writer; all other services call it via internal function calls, never write to audit_log directly).
backend.business_modules: IngestionEngine: parses ISO 20022 XML (camt.053 statements) and SWIFT MT940 using python-mt940 and lxml libraries; normalizes to internal LedgerEntry format; stores to ledger_entries table; records SourceFileManifest with file hash and ingestion timestamp. MatchingEngine: loads unmatched LedgerEntry records for current reconciliation_date, applies matching rules (transaction_ref exact match, then amount + value_date proximity match within tolerance), posts balanced journal entries for matched pairs, marks unmatched items as exceptions. ExceptionRouter: classifies unmatched exceptions by root cause code (timing_difference, amount_mismatch, missing_counterparty, duplicate_detected), assigns to corresponding operations_queue based on routing rules in tolerance_thresholds config.
backend.background_jobs: Celery Beat schedule: IngestionTask at 02:00 UTC daily (triggered by S3 event on file arrival as backup trigger). MatchingTask at 03:15 UTC. ExceptionRoutingTask at 04:45 UTC. ReconciliationCloseTask at 05:15 UTC (generates attestation report PDF, stores to S3, sends completion notification). ArchivalTask at 01:00 UTC weekly (moves source files older than 90 days from S3 Standard to S3 Glacier).
backend.failure_handling: Each Celery task configured with max_retries=3, exponential backoff. IngestionTask failure: PagerDuty alert immediately (file ingestion is the critical path). MatchingTask partial failure: rollback matching results for the current run via Alembic transaction, retry from scratch. Dead letter queue in Redis for tasks exceeding max retries. On-call runbook linked in PagerDuty alert for manual fallback procedures.

security.auth_design: Keycloak realm with MFA enforced for all roles. JWT Bearer token validated by Flask middleware on every ExceptionQueueAPI request. Celery batch workers authenticate to PostgreSQL and Redis using AWS Secrets Manager credentials with 30-day rotation.
security.authorization_model: Keycloak roles: reconciliation-operator (GET exception queue, PATCH exception status to in-progress), reconciliation-approver (POST approval action, limited to exceptions not self-approved in same run), reconciliation-admin (CRUD tolerance thresholds and matching rules), audit-viewer (GET reconciliation runs and audit log). Dual-approver constraint: PostgreSQL trigger rejects second approval if approver_id matches first approval record.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: audit_log table is append-only enforced by PostgreSQL row-level security policy: INSERT allowed for audit_writer role, UPDATE and DELETE denied for all roles including superuser. Every exception queue action (view, claim, approve, reject), every matching run, and every configuration change writes an AuditRecord. Reconciliation attestation report generated per run includes run summary, matched count, exception count, approver names, and run timestamp for SOX Section 404 filing.
security.security_risks: Risk of operator approval collusion above the $10,000 dual-approver threshold. Mitigation: audit_log captures both approver identities; internal audit reviews monthly report of dual-approver exceptions for same-user patterns.

data.entities: ledger_entries (id UUID PK, reconciliation_date DATE, source_system ENUM core_banking/card_processor/payment_network, transaction_ref VARCHAR, amount NUMERIC(18,4), currency CHAR(3), value_date DATE, counterparty_account VARCHAR, raw_file_id UUID FK, match_status ENUM unmatched/matched/exception, matched_pair_id UUID, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). reconciliation_runs (id UUID PK, reconciliation_date DATE, status ENUM in_progress/matched/exceptions_pending/closed/failed, started_at, completed_at, matched_count INT, exception_count INT, attestation_report_s3_key VARCHAR). exception_items (id UUID PK, run_id UUID FK, ledger_entry_id UUID FK, exception_code ENUM timing_difference/amount_mismatch/missing_counterparty/duplicate_detected, amount NUMERIC(18,4), queue_id UUID FK, status ENUM open/in_progress/resolved/escalated, resolution_notes TEXT, closed_at TIMESTAMPTZ). source_file_manifests (id UUID PK, file_name, file_format ENUM iso20022/swift_mt940/csv, s3_key, file_hash_sha256, received_at, ingestion_status ENUM pending/ingested/failed). operations_queues (id UUID PK, queue_name VARCHAR, exception_codes JSONB, assigned_team VARCHAR, sla_hours INT). tolerance_thresholds (id UUID PK, source_system, exception_code, amount_tolerance NUMERIC, date_tolerance_days INT, updated_by, updated_at). approval_actions (id UUID PK, exception_id UUID FK, approver_id VARCHAR, action ENUM approved/rejected, approved_at TIMESTAMPTZ, notes TEXT).
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 on RDS Multi-AZ (us-east-1) with read replica for audit report queries. Table partitioning: ledger_entries partitioned by reconciliation_date (monthly range partitions) to keep active-day queries fast. S3 for source file storage and attestation report archives. S3 Intelligent-Tiering lifecycle rule: move to Glacier after 90 days.
data.consistency_notes: Matching engine runs inside a PostgreSQL transaction; if any matched pair write fails, the entire matching run is rolled back. Exception routing runs in a separate transaction after matching completes. Dual-approver constraint enforced by DB-level trigger on approval_actions insert.
data.data_risks: Schema migrations on ledger_entries (large partitioned table) must be tested on staging with production-scale data volumes before applying to production. Rolling migrations using Alembic with explicit rollback scripts required for all changes to ledger_entries, reconciliation_runs, and exception_items.

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate cluster in us-east-1 with three task definitions: reconciliation-batch-worker (Celery worker + Beat scheduler), exception-queue-api (Flask WSGI via gunicorn, behind ALB), audit-archival-worker (weekly S3 archival job). RDS PostgreSQL Multi-AZ in private subnet. ElastiCache Redis in private subnet. S3 buckets for source files and attestation reports. Secrets Manager for database credentials and Keycloak client secrets.
devops.environments: dev (single-AZ, t4g.medium RDS), staging (Multi-AZ RDS replica, production-scale seed data for performance testing), production (Multi-AZ RDS, Fargate auto-scaling 2-6 batch workers for end-of-month volume).
devops.ci_cd_design: Jenkins pipeline: flake8 + mypy lint, pytest unit and integration tests (90% coverage gate), Docker build, push to ECR, deploy to ECS via CloudFormation change set. Alembic migration applied as ECS one-off task before new app version starts. Rollback: previous ECR image tag redeployed via CloudFormation rollback.
devops.rollback_strategy: Each Alembic migration paired with a down-migration script. ECS task definition rollback to previous revision if health checks fail post-deploy. If batch run produces unexpected exception count spike, reconciliation-admin can pause the run via admin CLI and roll back matching results using the stored transaction snapshot.
devops.devops_risks: End-of-month batch volume (3x normal) can exhaust Fargate task capacity if auto-scaling is not pre-warmed. Schedule a manual scale-out to 6 workers at 01:00 UTC on end-of-month dates. RDS failover during batch run would abort in-progress matching transaction; Celery retry logic handles rescheduling but manual monitoring is required on failover nights.

ISS-001.title: Matching engine used SQL self-join approach that was too slow to meet 06:00 UTC deadline at production volume
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 MatchingEngine loaded ledger entries via SQL self-join correlated subquery. Performance testing in round 2 showed it would take 9 hours for end-of-month volumes, far exceeding the 06:00 UTC deadline.

history.detail: Replaced with in-memory hash-map matching on transaction_ref, then amount+date proximity fallback

ISS-002.title: Dual-approver constraint was only enforced at application layer, not at database level
ISS-002.detail: Rounds 1-2 dual-approver enforcement was a Python check in the Flask route. SOX auditors require a database-level constraint that cannot be bypassed by application bugs or direct DB access.

history.detail: Dual-approver was an if-statement in Flask route
PostgreSQL UNIQUE constraint added but did not prevent same-user approval
PostgreSQL trigger on approval_actions rejects second approval if approver_id matches first approval on same exception_id

ISS-003.title: Attestation report format not specified; internal audit rejected round 2 report as insufficient for SOX Section 404
ISS-003.detail: Round 3 attestation report was a raw JSON export. Internal audit requires a structured PDF with: reconciliation date, source file manifest hashes, matched count, exception count by type, approver names and timestamps for each resolved exception, and sign-off attestation signature block.

history.detail: Basic JSON export added but rejected by audit
Attestation report format upgraded to structured text but still not meeting SOX PDF attestation requirements

ISS-004.title: End-of-month Fargate capacity spike not addressed in deployment_and_operations
ISS-004.detail: Round 3 plan did not describe the end-of-month batch volume (3x normal) and the corresponding pre-warm procedure for Fargate task count.

history.detail: Added manual scale-out to 6 workers at 01:00 UTC on end-of-month dates and monitoring procedures

focus_issues.title: Attestation report format not specified; internal audit rejected round 2 report as insufficient for SOX Section 404
focus_issues.detail: Internal audit requires a structured PDF attestation report with specific fields: reconciliation date, source file manifest hashes, matched/exception counts by type, approver names and timestamps, and a sign-off attestation block.

latest_recommendations: Specify attestation report as a PDF generated via WeasyPrint or ReportLab from a structured data model, stored to S3 per reconciliation run, containing: reconciliation date, source file names and SHA-256 hashes, matched entry count, exception count by exception_code, table of resolved exceptions with approver_id and approval_timestamp for each, and an attestation statement with the reconciliation-admin username and timestamp
Include the attestation report S3 key in the reconciliation_runs table so audit-viewer role can retrieve reports by run ID
revision_memory.latest_plan_title: GL Reconciliation Automation Tool — Round 3

convergence_state.reason: ISS-003 high severity unresolved after 3 rounds with audit rejection

previous_audits.summary: Strong round with ISS-001 matching performance and ISS-002 dual-approver database constraint resolved. ISS-003 attestation report remains the blocking issue — audit-rejected JSON format still not replaced with SOX-compliant PDF.
strengths: In-memory hash-map matching engine design correctly addresses 06:00 UTC completion deadline for end-of-month volumes
PostgreSQL trigger for dual-approver constraint satisfies SOX segregation-of-duties requirement
concerns: Attestation report is still described as a structured text file, not a PDF meeting SOX Section 404 requirements
Report does not include approver names and timestamps for resolved exceptions as required by internal audit
blocking_issues: SOX Section 404 attestation report must be a PDF with specific audit-required fields including source file hashes, exception approver identities, and attestation sign-off block
recommendations: Generate attestation report as PDF using WeasyPrint or ReportLab from reconciliation_runs, exception_items, and approval_actions data

Include: reconciliation_date, source file names and SHA-256 hashes from source_file_manifests, matched_count, exception_count by exception_code, resolved exception table with approver_id and approval_timestamp, attestation statement signed by reconciliation-admin identity and timestamp
Store PDF to S3 with path reconciliation-reports/{date}/attestation_{run_id}.pdf and record S3 key in reconciliation_runs.attestation_report_s3_key

issue_updates.title: Attestation report format insufficient for SOX Section 404
issue_updates.detail: Report described as structured text file, missing PDF format and approver identity table required by internal audit

previous_plan.matching_approach: In-memory hash-map matching: load LedgerEntry records for reconciliation_date into memory, match on transaction_ref exact match first, then amount+value_date proximity match within tolerance thresholds. Write matched pairs and unmatched exceptions in single PostgreSQL transaction.

ISS-001 (matching performance), ISS-002 (dual-approver DB constraint), and ISS-004 (end-of-month scaling) are resolved. The attestation report fix requires specifying the PDF generation library (WeasyPrint), the data sources (reconciliation_runs, exception_items, approval_actions, source_file_manifests), the required fields, and the S3 storage path. The plan quality is moderate — all core sections are strong but the observability section could be more detailed on specific reconciliation metrics.
