I will add adverse_action_notices as a first-class entity in data_model, add notice generation and delivery to the decisioning service workflow, and specify the remote-types CI check and shared interface package strategy in deployment_and_operations. Added Adverse Action Notice workflow to workflows section: on credit_decision.decision=declined, decisioning-svc generates notice within 5 minutes, maps bureau reason codes to ECOA statement codes, queues via SQS for email/portal delivery, and stamps sent_at. Updated functional_feature_map and system_components to include AdverseActionService within decisioning-svc.

Each remote CI pipeline validates against latest contracts package before deploy. Shell CI runs remote-types check pulling type stubs from each remote's published types artifact.

target_output.title: Personal Loan Portal -- Micro-Frontend Lending Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A micro-frontend personal loan self-service portal built with React 18 and Module Federation, backed by five domain-aligned NestJS microservices on AWS ECS Fargate. Socure KYC identity verification, real-time Experian bureau decisioning with 8-second SLA, DocuSign e-signature, Plaid ACH disbursement with NACHA deadline scheduling, and ECOA-compliant adverse action notice generation. Regulation Z, ECOA, FCRA, and NACHA compliant.
target_output.architecture_overview: React 18 Module Federation shell (S3 + CloudFront) dynamically loads four independently deployable micro-frontend remotes: @loan/application (multi-step form), @loan/kyc (Socure SDK embed), @loan/offers (offer card selection), and @loan/esign (DocuSign embedded signing). Shared @loan/contracts TypeScript package pins remote API surface. Five NestJS microservices on ECS Fargate behind internal ALB orchestrated by application-svc: identity-svc (Socure KYC), decisioning-svc (bureau pull, credit decision, adverse action notices), documents-svc (DocuSign webhooks), disbursement-svc (Plaid ACH with NACHA cutoff scheduler). Each service has its own Postgres 16 database. Redis 7 for session tokens, idempotency keys, and 24-hour bureau response cache. Cognito user pool handles email OTP for new applicants and SMS MFA for returning customers. Datadog APM traces propagated across all five services via x-datadog-trace-id headers.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: React 18, TypeScript, Module Federation (Webpack 5), Tailwind CSS, Zustand (shell state), React Query (per-remote API calls). Backend: Node.js 20, NestJS, TypeORM, class-validator, Bull queues (SQS adapter). Database: PostgreSQL 16 per service on RDS. Cache: Redis 7 (ElastiCache). Infrastructure: ECS Fargate, ALB, CloudFront, S3, SQS FIFO, Cognito, Secrets Manager, WAF. Integrations: Socure for KYC, Experian Connect+ for bureau, DocuSign eSignature API, Plaid Transfer for ACH. Observability: Datadog APM + Fullstory.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Loan Application Form (@loan/application MFE): purpose selection, loan amount slider ($1K-$50K), personal info, employment and income, consent to credit pull. Identity Verification (@loan/kyc MFE): Socure SDK embed for document scan and selfie liveness; status polling until kyc_status=pass. Offer Presentation (@loan/offers MFE): personalised offer cards with APR (in TILA-compliant format), term months, monthly payment; compare up to 3 offers. E-Signature (@loan/esign MFE): DocuSign embedded signing with Truth in Lending disclosure as first document. Status Dashboard: disbursement timeline, expected settlement date, loan account link. Adverse Action Notices: ECOA-compliant notice with FCRA reason codes delivered via portal and email within 30 days of declined decision. AdverseActionService within decisioning-svc handles generation, reason code mapping, and SQS-queued delivery.
target_output.system_components: Module Federation Shell (React 18 on CloudFront): bootstraps Cognito auth, mounts remotes via remote entry URLs stored in shell config. @loan/contracts package (internal npm): TypeScript interfaces for MFE props contracts. application-svc (Fargate): loan application CRUD, orchestration of identity, decisioning, document, and disbursement services. identity-svc (Fargate): Socure KYC API client, identity_verifications table, KYC status polling webhook. decisioning-svc (Fargate): Experian Connect+ bureau pull, credit_decisions, loan_offers, AdverseActionService (notice generation + SQS delivery), 24-hour Redis bureau cache keyed by SSN-hash. documents-svc (Fargate): DocuSign envelope creation, webhook handler for envelope completion, esignature_envelopes table. disbursement-svc (Fargate): Plaid Transfer ACH initiation, NACHA deadline scheduler (SQS FIFO with DelaySeconds=0, processed at 2:30 PM EST), disbursement_instructions table. External ALB + WAF for public traffic. Internal ALB for service-to-service.
target_output.workflows: Loan Application to Offer: Applicant completes @loan/application form and submits. application-svc creates loan_application{status=submitted} and calls identity-svc. identity-svc initiates Socure KYC; on webhook callback with status=pass, calls application-svc to advance status=identity_verified and trigger decisioning. decisioning-svc checks Redis bureau cache by SSN-hash; on cache miss calls Experian Connect+ (must return within 6 seconds or circuit-breaks to async path), persists credit_decisions, generates loan_offers, advances application status=decisioned. @loan/offers MFE polls GET /applications/:id/offers. Adverse Action Notice Flow: On credit_decision.decision=declined, AdverseActionService within decisioning-svc maps bureau FCRA reason codes to ECOA principal reason statements, creates adverse_action_notices{reason_codes, ecoa_deadline=decision_date+30days, delivery_method=email_and_portal}, publishes to SQS adverse-action-queue. SQS consumer sends SES email with CFPB model notice format and marks sent_at. Applicant views notice at /adverse-action/:noticeId with download link. E-Sign to Disbursement: Applicant accepts offer in @loan/offers, application-svc calls documents-svc to create DocuSign envelope with TILA disclosure + promissory note. On DocuSign envelope.completed webhook, documents-svc notifies application-svc which calls disbursement-svc. disbursement-svc creates disbursement_instructions and enqueues to NACHA-scheduler SQS FIFO queue; at 2:30 PM EST processes queue, initiates Plaid Transfer ACH, records ach_trace_number, sets scheduled_date=today if before cutoff else next business day.
target_output.data_model: loan_applications (id UUID PK, applicant_id, cognito_sub, loan_amount_cents, loan_purpose ENUM{debt_consolidation,home_improvement,medical,other}, employment_status, annual_income_cents, status ENUM{draft,submitted,identity_verified,decisioned,offer_selected,esigned,disbursed,declined,withdrawn}, created_at, updated_at). identity_verifications (id, application_id FK, socure_transaction_id, kyc_status ENUM{pending,pass,fail,refer}, kyc_score_json, completed_at). credit_decisions (id, application_id FK, bureau_pull_id, experian_report_ref_encrypted, decision ENUM{approved,declined,referred}, apr_bps, term_months, max_amount_cents, adverse_action_reason_codes TEXT[], decided_at). loan_offers (id, credit_decision_id FK, selected_amount_cents, selected_term_months, monthly_payment_cents, tila_apr_disclosed, offered_at, accepted_at). adverse_action_notices (id, application_id FK, credit_decision_id FK, reason_codes TEXT[], ecoa_reason_statements TEXT[], delivery_method ENUM{email_and_portal,postal}, ecoa_deadline DATE, sent_at, portal_viewed_at). esignature_envelopes (id, application_id FK, docusign_envelope_id, status ENUM{created,sent,signed,voided}, signed_at). disbursement_instructions (id, application_id FK, plaid_processor_token_ref, amount_cents, ach_trace_number, scheduled_date DATE, status ENUM{queued,submitted,settled,failed}, settled_at). applicant_profiles (id, cognito_sub, ssn_encrypted, dob, address_json, phone_encrypted, created_at).
target_output.api_design: application-svc: POST /api/v1/applications (create draft), PATCH /api/v1/applications/:id (update form fields), POST /api/v1/applications/:id/submit (trigger KYC + decisioning), GET /api/v1/applications/:id (status + current step), GET /api/v1/applications/:id/offers (offer list), POST /api/v1/applications/:id/offers/:offerId/accept. decisioning-svc (internal): POST /internal/decisions (called by application-svc), GET /internal/decisions/:applicationId. AdverseActionService internal: POST /internal/adverse-action (called on declined decision). documents-svc: POST /internal/envelopes (create DocuSign envelope), POST /webhooks/docusign (DocuSign connect webhook). disbursement-svc: POST /internal/disbursements (enqueue ACH), GET /internal/disbursements/:applicationId. Adverse action portal: GET /api/v1/adverse-action/:noticeId. All public endpoints require Cognito JWT. Internal endpoints require service-to-service HMAC signing with shared key in Secrets Manager.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Cognito user pool: email OTP for new applicants (SRP auth flow), password + SMS MFA for returning users. JWT access tokens 15-minute expiry; refresh tokens 7-day expiry. WAF on CloudFront and external ALB: SQL injection, XSS, rate limit 200 req/min per IP, geo-block non-US traffic. SSN and bank account numbers encrypted with AES-256-GCM at application layer before insert; keys in AWS Secrets Manager with 90-day rotation. Experian bureau pull response encrypted at rest in Redis with separate KMS key. Plaid processor tokens stored in Secrets Manager, not application database. Service-to-service authentication via HMAC SHA-256 with shared secret from Secrets Manager. TILA APR disclosure stamped on loan offers per Regulation Z. ECOA adverse action notices generated for all declined decisions within 30 days via AdverseActionService. FCRA permissible purpose logged per bureau pull. NACHA ACH rules: prenote validation for first disbursement to new bank account.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Five ECS Fargate services deployed via GitHub Actions rolling update (min 50% healthy, max 200% during deploy). Each microservice has its own GitHub Actions workflow: lint, typecheck, unit test (Jest 90% coverage gate), integration test (Postgres + Redis via Testcontainers), Docker build + ECR push, ECS task definition update, CloudWatch health check gate. Micro-frontend remotes deployed to S3 prefix per remote (e.g., s3://loan-mfe-prod/kyc/) with CloudFront cache invalidation on deploy. @loan/contracts TypeScript package published to internal npm registry on tag; each remote CI validates against latest contracts version before deploy. Shell CI runs remote-types check by importing type stubs from each remote's types artifact. Environments: dev (single-AZ Fargate, shared dev Postgres), staging (multi-AZ, mirrored production scale for load testing), production (us-east-1a and us-east-1c, auto-scaling 2-10 tasks per service on CPU 60% target). Secrets rotation: Secrets Manager 90-day auto-rotation for encryption keys, 30-day for service HMAC keys.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM with trace context propagated via x-datadog-trace-id and x-datadog-parent-id headers across all five services. Custom dashboards: decisioning latency p50/p95/p99 (SLA line at 8s), bureau cache hit rate (target >40%), ACH submission success rate, adverse action notice sent within 24h rate, funnel conversion per step (from Fullstory events). PagerDuty alerts: decisioning p99 >6 seconds, ACH failure rate >1%, adverse action notice delivery failure, Socure KYC error rate >5%.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $4,200/month at 1,000 daily applications on production. Fargate: 5 services at 0.5 vCPU/1GB each scaling to 2-10 tasks. RDS Postgres: 5 db.t4g.medium instances ($600/month combined). Bureau response caching at 40% hit rate saves approximately $2,000/month in Experian inquiry fees at $0.35/pull. CloudFront CDN for static MFE bundles reduces origin load. Fullstory session replay licensed separately. Cost optimisation: Fargate Spot for dev/staging, reserved capacity for production baseline.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (weeks 1-10): loan application form, Socure KYC integration, Experian bureau pull with offer generation, DocuSign e-sign, manual ACH disbursement trigger, ECOA adverse action notice generation and SES delivery, Cognito auth with OTP, Regulation Z TILA disclosures on offers, GitHub Actions CI per service and MFE, dev and staging environments. Phase 2 (weeks 11-16): Plaid Transfer ACH with NACHA scheduler, automated prenote validation, returning customer SMS MFA, bureau response Redis cache optimisation, Fullstory funnel analytics, production go-live with 500-applicant pilot. Phase 3 (weeks 17-24): Module Federation contract versioning with @loan/contracts package, cross-remote CI types check, HELOC product line on shared shell, real-time income verification via Argyle payroll API, mobile responsive polish.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode across all services and MFEs. ESLint + Prettier gated in CI. Jest 90% coverage for microservice business logic. Cypress E2E for full application-to-offer and e-sign flows in staging. All API contracts documented with OpenAPI 3.0; breaking changes require version bump. @loan/contracts package: semver patch for additive interface changes, minor for new optional props, major for breaking changes. MFE remotes must not import from each other -- only from @loan/contracts and their own backend API. Secrets never in environment variables in application code -- all via Secrets Manager SDK calls.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Experian bureau API timeout exceeding 8-second SLA during peak hours. Mitigation: circuit breaker with async decision fallback path; p95 monitoring with alert at 6 seconds. Risk: Plaid Transfer ACH failure after DocuSign completion leaves applicant in signed-but-not-funded state. Mitigation: disbursement-svc saga with compensating rollback notification; applicant status shows pending_disbursement with support contact. Risk: Module Federation remote deployment breaking shell before @loan/contracts CI check is in place. Mitigation: implement contracts check in Phase 1 CI, not deferred to Phase 3. Tradeoff: database-per-service over shared Postgres increases infrastructure cost but is required for audit isolation between decisioning and disbursement services per compliance requirement.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: ECOA adverse action notices added as first-class feature in decisioning-svc with 30-day deadline tracking and SES delivery. Module Federation contract versioning via @loan/contracts package confirmed for Phase 1. Bureau pull provider confirmed as Experian Connect+ over TransUnion due to existing bank data sharing agreement. Plaid Transfer selected over direct ACH processor for bank account verification plus transfer in single integration. Prenote validation required for first disbursement per NACHA rules.

metadata.notes: Round 2 personal loan micro-frontend portal; adds ECOA adverse action workflow and MFE contract versioning strategy.

profile.domain: Community Bank Regulatory Compliance -- HMDA Reporting

project_goal.value: Web application for compliance officers to assemble, validate, and submit annual HMDA Loan Application Register filings to the CFPB HMDA Platform
project_goal.rationale: Community bank must file HMDA LAR annually under Regulation C; current process is manual spreadsheet-based

target_users.value: Compliance officers and loan data analysts at a community bank; typically 2-5 users per institution
target_users.rationale: Regulatory filing workflow users with deep HMDA domain knowledge but limited technical skills

project_class.rationale: Browser-based tool for form assembly, validation, and CFPB API submission

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, auth, data, admin_panel, batch_jobs
capabilities.rationale: Vue 3 frontend, FastAPI backend, Azure AD auth, Postgres data store, admin panel for rule management, batch import and validation jobs

complexity_level.rationale: Batch import, CFPB edit check rules engine, and external CFPB API integration add moderate complexity to a primarily CRUD application

risk_level.rationale: Incorrect HMDA filing carries civil money penalties from CFPB; LAR contains borrower PII including race, sex, income, and census tract

data_sensitivity.rationale: HMDA LAR contains borrower demographic data (race, ethnicity, sex, age) and financial data (loan amount, income, property address) classified as personal

external_exposure.rationale: Internal compliance tool; all access requires Azure AD authentication

access_model.value: Azure AD SSO with RBAC: compliance_officer (full access), loan_analyst (read + data entry), auditor (read-only)
access_model.rationale: Role-based access required to prevent unauthorized LAR submission or modification

feature_scope.value: Batch import from loan origination system CSV export, HMDA LAR field mapper, CFPB edit check validation engine, error remediation interface, compliance officer sign-off workflow, CFPB HMDA Platform API submission, submission receipt and audit trail storage, historical filing archive
feature_scope.rationale: Full HMDA filing workflow from raw loan data through CFPB submission

mvp_scope.value: CSV batch import, HMDA LAR field mapping, CFPB edit check validation with error display, compliance officer sign-off, CFPB API submission, receipt storage
mvp_scope.rationale: Core filing workflow needed for Q4 regulatory deadline

security_baseline.value: Azure AD SSO, TLS 1.3, Postgres encryption at rest via Azure disk encryption, audit log for all LAR data access and modification, no LAR data cached in browser
security_baseline.rationale: PII handling requires access control and audit trail; no browser-side storage of sensitive data

frontend_stack.value: Vue 3 with TypeScript and Vite
frontend_stack.rationale: Bank engineering team expertise in Vue; Vite for fast builds

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI and SQLAlchemy 2.0
backend_stack.rationale: Python for data processing batch jobs; FastAPI for async API endpoints

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Database for PostgreSQL
data_platform.rationale: Managed Postgres on Azure; relational schema suits LAR structured data

hosting_target.value: Azure App Service (Python 3.12 runtime) with Azure Static Web Apps for Vue frontend
hosting_target.rationale: Bank is Azure-first; App Service suits low-concurrent compliance workflow

privacy_retention_policy.value: LAR data retained 3 years per Regulation C; CFPB submission receipts retained 5 years; audit logs retained 7 years per bank policy
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Regulation C and bank record retention policies set these minimums

future_scope.value: CRA data reporting module, multi-institution support for bank subsidiaries, automated LAR pre-population from loan origination system API rather than CSV export
future_scope.rationale: Bank plans to extend same platform for CRA filings and multi-branch roll-out

constraints.value: CFPB HMDA Platform API rate limit: 10 submissions per day per institution; annual filing deadline is March 1; system must support full LAR re-upload if submission is rejected
constraints.rationale: CFPB API rate limit constrains submission retry strategy; March 1 deadline is hard regulatory cutoff

observability_baseline.value: Azure Monitor Application Insights for API traces; alerts on batch job failure and CFPB submission error response
observability_baseline.rationale: Azure-native monitoring consistent with bank Azure environment

execution_preference.value: Waterfall-style delivery with fixed milestones due to regulatory deadline; Q3 development, Q4 UAT, Q1 production before March 1 filing
execution_preference.rationale: Regulatory deadline does not permit flexible scope; fixed deliverable milestones required

compliance_context.value: HMDA Regulation C (12 CFR Part 1003), CFPB HMDA Platform submission requirements, Dodd-Frank Section 1094, bank internal data governance policy for PII handling
compliance_context.rationale: HMDA compliance is the primary regulatory driver; Dodd-Frank governs CFPB authority over HMDA

project.goal: HMDA LAR assembly, validation, and CFPB submission tool
project.target_users: Compliance officers and loan analysts

backend.framework: FastAPI with SQLAlchemy 2.0 and Alembic migrations

security.auth: Azure AD SSO via MSAL
security.encryption: TLS 1.3 in transit, Azure disk encryption at rest
security.compliance: Regulation C, CFPB HMDA Platform rules

data.db: PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Database for PostgreSQL

devops.hosting: Azure App Service + Azure Static Web Apps

confirmed_decisions.edit_check_source: CFPB published edit check rules CSV

reasoner_reviews.product: The HMDA LAR filing workflow is well-scoped for MVP. The edit check validation engine is the most complex component -- CFPB publishes over 100 syntactical and validity edit checks that must all be implemented. The compliance officer sign-off step is a critical audit control that must be logged immutably.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: FastAPI with async endpoints is a good fit for the file upload and batch processing workflow. The CFPB edit check rules should be loaded from a database table (edit_check_rules) to allow rule updates without code deployment. SQLAlchemy 2.0 async session is appropriate for the PostgreSQL workload.
reasoner_reviews.security: Azure AD SSO provides appropriate authentication for internal compliance staff. Audit logging of every LAR record modification is mandatory given the PII sensitivity and regulatory stakes. LAR borrower data should not be logged in Application Insights traces to avoid PII leakage.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The CFPB API rate limit of 10 submissions per day means the system must prevent accidental resubmission. An idempotency check on submission_batch before calling CFPB API is essential. March 1 deadline is a hard constraint -- staging UAT must complete by mid-January.

backend.service_design: FastAPI application with modules: import_module (CSV upload + LAR field mapping), validation_module (edit check rules engine), submission_module (CFPB API client), audit_module (append-only audit_log). Background tasks via FastAPI BackgroundTasks for batch import and validation jobs.
backend.api_patterns: RESTful endpoints under /api/v1. POST /batches/import (upload CSV), GET /batches/:id/status (import progress), POST /batches/:id/validate (trigger edit checks), GET /batches/:id/errors (validation error list), POST /batches/:id/signoff (compliance officer approval), POST /batches/:id/submit (CFPB submission), GET /submissions/:id/receipt.
backend.failure_handling: CFPB submission failure stores error response in cfpb_submission_receipts.response_body. Rate limit exceeded returns 429 with retry_after header. Batch import failures stored per-row in validation_errors table.
backend.background_jobs: AsyncIO background task for CSV parsing and LAR field mapping. Edit check validation runs as background job with progress stored in submission_batches.validation_status.

frontend.app_structure: Vue 3 SPA with Pinia state and Vue Router. Three main views: BatchImport (drag-drop CSV upload), ValidationReview (error list with inline field edit), SubmissionDashboard (sign-off and CFPB submission status).
frontend.pages_and_flows: Login via MSAL redirect to Azure AD. ImportBatch page: upload CSV, view mapping preview, trigger import. ValidationView: paginated error table with CFPB edit check code, LAR row reference, current value, and remediation guidance. Sign-off page: summary stats, compliance officer sign-off button with confirmation modal. SubmissionStatus: CFPB receipt display with acknowledgment token.
frontend.state_management: Pinia store for current batch state (batch_id, validation_status, error_count, sign_off_status). Vue Query for polling import and validation progress.

security.auth_design: Azure AD SSO via MSAL.js in Vue SPA. Access token passed as Authorization header to FastAPI. FastAPI validates JWT via azure-ad-jwt-validator. Token expiry 1 hour; refresh via MSAL silent token acquisition.
security.authorization_model: RBAC enforced in FastAPI route decorators: compliance_officer can call signoff and submit endpoints; loan_analyst can call import and validate; auditor has GET-only access. Azure AD groups mapped to roles via token claims.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: append-only audit_log table records: user_id, action ENUM{import,validate,edit_field,signoff,submit}, batch_id, lar_row_id (nullable), before_value_hash, after_value_hash, timestamp. No raw PII values in audit log -- only field name and value hash.

data.entities: loan_application_records (id, batch_id FK, uli, action_taken, loan_type, property_type, loan_purpose, occupancy, loan_amount_thousands, census_tract, applicant_race, applicant_sex, income, status ENUM{raw,validated,error,approved}), hmda_lar_rows (derived view joining loan_application_records with validation status), submission_batches (id, institution_lei, reporting_year, import_status, validation_status, signoff_user_id, signoff_at, submitted_at), validation_errors (id, batch_id, lar_row_id, edit_check_code, field_name, current_value, error_message, resolved_at), cfpb_submission_receipts (id, batch_id, submission_timestamp, cfpb_response_code, acknowledgment_token, response_body_json, filing_year), edit_check_rules (id, check_code, check_type ENUM{syntactical,validity,quality,macro}, field_name, rule_expression, error_message, effective_date, deprecated_at).
data.schema_notes: loan_application_records stores raw LAR field values as strings to preserve exact values from loan origination export. Numeric fields validated during edit check phase, not at import.
data.data_risks: HMDA LAR fields include race, ethnicity, sex, and age -- demographic data with strict PII handling requirements. Row-level access control not required (small user base) but field-level audit logging is mandatory.

devops.deployment_topology: Azure App Service (Python 3.12, B2 tier) for FastAPI backend. Azure Static Web Apps for Vue 3 frontend. Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server (General Purpose, 4 vCores). Azure Blob Storage for uploaded CSV files. Azure Key Vault for FastAPI secrets.
devops.ci_cd_design: Azure DevOps Pipelines: Python lint (ruff), mypy type check, pytest (unit + integration with Postgres), Docker build, App Service deploy via az webapp deploy. Vue pipeline: ESLint, Vitest unit tests, vite build, Static Web Apps CLI deploy.
devops.observability_stack: Azure Monitor Application Insights SDK in FastAPI (opencensus-ext-azure). Custom metrics: batch_import_duration_seconds, validation_error_count, cfpb_submission_status. Alerts: batch job failure, CFPB submission 4xx/5xx response.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a HMDA regulatory filing tool for a community bank. Key decisions: FastAPI backend with async batch processing for CSV import and CFPB edit checks, edit_check_rules stored in database for non-deployment rule updates, Azure AD RBAC for compliance officer / analyst / auditor separation. The plan is intentionally weak quality -- several sections are thin, reflecting the 15% weak quality distribution target.
target_output.title: HMDA LAR Filing Portal
target_output.executive_summary: A private web application for community bank compliance officers to import loan origination data, run CFPB edit check validation, and submit annual HMDA Loan Application Register filings. Built with Vue 3 and FastAPI on Azure. Handles borrower PII under Regulation C with Azure AD access control and append-only audit logging.
target_output.architecture_overview: Vue 3 SPA on Azure Static Web Apps communicates via HTTPS to a FastAPI backend on Azure App Service. Backend connects to PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Database for PostgreSQL. CSV uploads stored temporarily in Azure Blob Storage. FastAPI background tasks handle batch import and CFPB edit check validation jobs. Azure AD SSO via MSAL.js authenticates all users. CFPB HMDA Platform API called synchronously during submission step. No caching layer; small user base (2-5 concurrent) does not require Redis.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Vue 3, TypeScript, Vite, Pinia, MSAL.js for Azure AD. Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, SQLAlchemy 2.0 async, Alembic migrations, Pydantic v2. Database: PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server. Storage: Azure Blob Storage for CSV uploads. Hosting: Azure App Service (B2 tier) + Azure Static Web Apps. CI/CD: Azure DevOps Pipelines. Monitoring: Azure Monitor Application Insights.
target_output.functional_feature_map: CSV batch import with LAR field mapping from loan origination export. CFPB edit check validation engine running 100+ syntactical and validity checks against edit_check_rules table. Inline error remediation interface for loan analysts. Compliance officer sign-off workflow with immutable audit record. CFPB HMDA Platform API submission with receipt storage. Historical filing archive by reporting year.
target_output.system_components: Vue 3 SPA handling batch import flow, validation review, and submission dashboard. FastAPI backend with import, validation, submission, and audit modules. PostgreSQL 16 storing LAR rows, validation errors, submission receipts, edit check rules. Azure Blob Storage for uploaded CSV files. Azure AD for authentication.
target_output.workflows: Batch Import and Validation: Compliance officer uploads loan origination system CSV export via drag-drop interface. FastAPI background task parses CSV, maps fields to HMDA LAR schema (universal loan identifier, action taken, loan type, census tract, applicant demographics), stores rows in loan_application_records. Validation job iterates rows against edit_check_rules table, creating validation_errors records with check code and field-level guidance. Analyst reviews error list and edits fields inline; each edit logged to audit_log with before/after value hashes. CFPB Submission: Compliance officer reviews validation summary (zero syntactical errors required, quality errors annotatable), clicks sign-off with MFA confirmation. FastAPI submission module calls CFPB HMDA Platform API POST /transmittal with LAR file. Response receipt stored in cfpb_submission_receipts. On rejection, error response displayed and reupload workflow initiated.
target_output.data_model: loan_application_records (id, batch_id, uli, action_taken, loan_type, property_type, loan_purpose, occupancy_type, loan_amount_thousands, census_tract, applicant_race, applicant_ethnicity, applicant_sex, income, status). submission_batches (id, institution_lei, reporting_year, import_status, validation_status, signoff_user_id, signoff_at, submitted_at). validation_errors (id, batch_id, lar_row_id, edit_check_code, field_name, current_value, error_message, resolved_at). cfpb_submission_receipts (id, batch_id, submission_timestamp, cfpb_response_code, acknowledgment_token, response_body_json). edit_check_rules (id, check_code, check_type, field_name, rule_expression, error_message, effective_date). audit_log (id, user_id, action, batch_id, lar_row_id, field_name, before_hash, after_hash, timestamp).
target_output.api_design: POST /api/v1/batches/import (multipart CSV upload, returns batch_id). GET /api/v1/batches/:id/status (import and validation progress). POST /api/v1/batches/:id/validate (trigger edit check job). GET /api/v1/batches/:id/errors (paginated validation error list). PATCH /api/v1/batches/:id/rows/:rowId (field edit with audit log). POST /api/v1/batches/:id/signoff (compliance officer approval). POST /api/v1/batches/:id/submit (CFPB submission, enforces daily rate limit check). GET /api/v1/submissions/:id/receipt. All endpoints require Azure AD JWT.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Azure AD SSO with MSAL.js. JWT validated in FastAPI via azure-ad-jwt-validator; token claims map to compliance_officer, loan_analyst, auditor roles. Only compliance_officer role can call signoff and submit endpoints. Append-only audit_log for all LAR modifications using before/after value hashes (no raw PII in logs). TLS 1.3 in transit. Azure disk encryption at rest. Azure Key Vault for FastAPI secrets. CFPB submission idempotency check prevents resubmission within same calendar day. HMDA Regulation C and Dodd-Frank Section 1094 compliance.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Azure DevOps Pipelines for CI/CD. Backend pipeline: ruff lint, mypy type check, pytest with Postgres testcontainer, Docker build, az webapp deploy to App Service. Frontend pipeline: ESLint, Vitest, vite build, SWA CLI deploy. Environments: dev (shared App Service B1, dev Postgres), staging (B2 tier, staging Postgres with sanitised data copy for UAT), production (B2 App Service, General Purpose 4-vCore Postgres). Database migrations via Alembic applied as pre-deploy step.
target_output.observability: Azure Monitor Application Insights SDK in FastAPI. Custom metrics: batch_import_duration_seconds, validation_error_count_per_batch, cfpb_submission_result{status}. Alerts: batch import failure, CFPB submission 4xx/5xx response, Azure AD auth failure spike. LAR row data excluded from Application Insights telemetry to prevent PII in traces.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Approximately $200/month on Azure. App Service B2 (~$70/month), Azure Database for PostgreSQL General Purpose 4-vCore (~$100/month), Static Web Apps free tier, Blob Storage under $5/month. Low concurrency (2-5 users) does not require scaling beyond B2. Cost review annually before filing season.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 Q3 (10 weeks): CSV import and LAR field mapper, CFPB edit check validation engine with 100+ rules loaded from edit_check_rules table, inline error remediation, compliance officer sign-off, Azure AD RBAC, Azure DevOps CI/CD, dev and staging environments. Phase 2 Q4 (4 weeks): CFPB HMDA Platform API submission integration, receipt and acknowledgment storage, reupload workflow for rejected submissions, UAT with compliance team. Phase 3 Q1 (2 weeks): Production go-live, March 1 filing, audit log reporting for compliance review, historical archive view.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python ruff for linting, mypy for type checking, pytest for unit and integration tests (70% coverage target). Vue ESLint + Vitest for frontend. Alembic migrations reviewed and approved before merging. Edit check rules loaded from database -- never hard-coded in Python. No LAR PII values in log statements or Application Insights custom events.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: CFPB edit check rules change annually; if edit_check_rules table is not updated before filing season, valid LAR rows may be rejected. Mitigation: CFPB publishes rule changes in October; compliance officer reviews and updates rules table before Q4 UAT. Risk: CFPB HMDA Platform API rejects LAR file after March 1 deadline. Mitigation: internal deadline of February 15 for final submission to allow one reupload cycle. Tradeoff: FastAPI BackgroundTasks over Celery reduces infrastructure complexity at the cost of no persistent task queue; acceptable given low submission frequency (once per year) and low concurrency.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Edit check rules stored in database over hard-coded configuration to allow annual updates without deployment. Azure Blob Storage selected over local filesystem for CSV uploads to support App Service restarts. Compliance officer sign-off implemented as database record with user_id and timestamp, not digital signature, per legal team guidance. No caching layer required at 2-5 concurrent users.

metadata.notes: Round 1 HMDA LAR filing portal; weak quality with thinner sections on observability and scaling, appropriate for compliance tool with low concurrency.

profile.domain: Banking Cloud Infrastructure Cost Optimisation

project_goal.value: Automation tool that analyses daily AWS cloud spend for lending workloads, identifies over-provisioned RDS instances and idle EC2 nodes, and generates right-sizing recommendations with projected monthly savings
project_goal.rationale: Bank FinOps team estimates 35% of lending infrastructure is over-provisioned; automated analysis replaces manual monthly review

target_users.value: FinOps engineers and cloud platform team managing lending workload AWS accounts
target_users.rationale: Internal technical audience consuming recommendations via weekly digest and approval portal

project_class.rationale: Scheduled Lambda functions with no user-facing UI beyond digest email and approval links

capabilities.rationale: Python Lambda functions, AWS Cost Explorer + CloudWatch integrations, batch analysis jobs, analytics for savings projections

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-source data ingestion (Cost Explorer + CloudWatch), utilisation percentile computation via pandas, approval workflow with automated resize via SSM

risk_level.rationale: Read-only analysis by default; resize actions require explicit human approval; no customer data processed

data_sensitivity.rationale: Cost and utilisation data is internal infrastructure telemetry; no customer PII

external_exposure.rationale: Lambda functions invoked by EventBridge; approval links in digest email are internal VPN-gated

access_model.value: IAM roles for Lambda execution; approval links use signed S3 pre-signed URLs with 24-hour expiry for one-click approval action
access_model.rationale: IAM role scoped to Cost Explorer read and SSM send-command for approved resize actions

feature_scope.value: Daily Lambda ingesting Cost Explorer and CloudWatch metrics, utilisation percentile analysis per resource, right-sizing recommendation generation ranked by savings, weekly digest email with top 10 recommendations, one-click approval triggering SSM automated resize, Postgres tracking of recommendations and approval records, historical savings realised reporting
feature_scope.rationale: End-to-end cost optimisation loop from detection through automated action

mvp_scope.value: Daily analysis Lambda, recommendations in Postgres, weekly digest email with top 10 by savings potential, approval tracking
mvp_scope.rationale: Core recommendation loop needed before automated resize actions in Phase 2

security_baseline.value: IAM roles with least-privilege (Cost Explorer read-only, CloudWatch GetMetricData, SSM SendCommand on tagged resources only), Lambda in VPC, Postgres credentials in Secrets Manager
security_baseline.rationale: Automated resize actions require strict IAM scope to prevent unintended infrastructure changes

frontend_stack.value: none
frontend_stack.rationale: automation_tool; no browser UI; approvals via email link only

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with boto3 and pandas for data processing Lambda functions
backend_stack.rationale: boto3 for AWS API calls, pandas for utilisation percentile computation across multi-day CloudWatch datasets

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 on RDS (db.t4g.micro) for recommendations and approval records
data_platform.rationale: Relational store for recommendation history; t4g.micro appropriate for low-volume internal tool

hosting_target.value: AWS Lambda with EventBridge Scheduler; one daily analysis function and one weekly digest function
hosting_target.rationale: Serverless scheduling eliminates always-on infrastructure cost for a batch analysis tool

privacy_retention_policy.value: Cost and utilisation snapshots retained 13 months for year-over-year comparison; recommendations retained 2 years; approval records retained indefinitely for audit
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: 13-month retention enables year-over-year savings comparison; audit records kept permanently

future_scope.value: Automated scheduled resize without human approval for low-risk resource classes (e.g., dev RDS instances), Savings Plans purchase recommendations, cross-account analysis across all bank AWS accounts
future_scope.rationale: Full FinOps automation roadmap after manual approval workflow is trusted

constraints.value: Cost Explorer API has 1-day data lag; CloudWatch detailed monitoring must be enabled on target resources (additional $0.01/metric/month cost); Lambda must complete analysis within 15-minute timeout
constraints.rationale: AWS API constraints and Lambda timeout bound the analysis window per invocation

observability_baseline.value: AWS CloudWatch Logs for Lambda execution; CloudWatch Alarms on Lambda error rate and duration approaching timeout; SNS alert on analysis job failure
observability_baseline.rationale: CloudWatch is the natural monitoring choice for Lambda-based tools in the bank AWS environment

execution_preference.value: Single-developer project; GitHub Actions CI; SAM or CDK for infrastructure-as-code deployment
execution_preference.rationale: FinOps engineer maintains tool independently; IaC ensures reproducible Lambda and EventBridge deployment

project.goal: Automated lending workload cloud cost analysis and right-sizing recommendation tool
project.target_users: FinOps engineers and cloud platform team

backend.framework: boto3 for AWS APIs, pandas for utilisation analysis, psycopg2 for Postgres, SES for digest email

security.auth: IAM roles for Lambda execution

data.storage: no S3 for intermediate data in MVP

reasoner_reviews.product: The tool's value proposition is clear: replace manual monthly FinOps reviews with daily automated analysis. The one-click approval-to-resize loop is the differentiating feature -- without it the tool is just a reporting dashboard. The weekly digest email format with top 10 recommendations ranked by savings is the right UX for a busy platform team.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Splitting into two Lambda functions (daily analysis, weekly digest) is correct -- they have different cadence and memory requirements. The pandas utilisation percentile computation needs to handle 14-day CloudWatch datasets per resource; at 500 resources this could approach Lambda memory limits. Consider chunking resource analysis into batches of 100 to stay within 1GB Lambda memory and 15-minute timeout.
reasoner_reviews.security: The IAM role for the resize Lambda must be scoped to SSM SendCommand only on EC2 instances and RDS instances tagged with cost-optimisation-eligible=true. This prevents accidental resize of production databases. The approval link pre-signed URL should be single-use -- invalidated in Postgres after first approval click.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Lambda 15-minute timeout with 500 lending workload resources is tight. At 300ms per CloudWatch GetMetricData call, 500 resources takes 150 seconds for metric fetch alone. Implement concurrent boto3 calls using ThreadPoolExecutor (CloudWatch API allows 50 req/s). Analyse only resources with workload_tags matching lending account IDs to reduce scope.

backend.service_design: Two Lambda functions: analysis_lambda (daily at 06:00 UTC) ingests Cost Explorer GetCostAndUsage and CloudWatch GetMetricData for tagged RDS and EC2 resources, computes p50/p95 CPU and memory utilisation over 14-day window via pandas, writes right_sizing_recommendations to Postgres. digest_lambda (weekly Monday 08:00 UTC) queries top 10 recommendations by projected_monthly_savings_usd DESC, generates HTML digest, sends via SES. approval_lambda (triggered by pre-signed URL click stored in API Gateway) validates approval token, marks recommendation approved, triggers SSM automation document for resize.
backend.business_modules: UtilisationAnalyser: fetches CloudWatch CPUUtilization, DatabaseConnections, FreeableMemory per resource over 14 days, computes p50/p95 with pandas DataFrame, determines right-size tier from AWS instance family lookup table. SavingsProjector: computes projected_monthly_savings as (current_instance_hourly_price - recommended_instance_hourly_price) * 730 from EC2/RDS pricing table. RecommendationWriter: upserts right_sizing_recommendations rows; marks previous recommendations for same resource_id as superseded.
backend.failure_handling: analysis_lambda wraps each resource analysis in try/except; per-resource failures logged to CloudWatch and stored in resource_utilization_snapshots with error_flag=True. digest_lambda falls back to last successful recommendations if current analysis failed. SSM resize failure stored in approval_records.resize_outcome with error detail.

security.auth_design: analysis_lambda IAM role: Cost Explorer GetCostAndUsage read-only, CloudWatch GetMetricData read-only on all resources, RDS Describe, EC2 Describe. approval_lambda IAM role: SSM SendCommand restricted to resources with tag cost-optimisation-eligible=true, RDS ModifyDBInstance for storage/instance class only. Separate IAM roles for read and write actions.
security.secrets_management: RDS Postgres connection string in Secrets Manager. Fetched at Lambda cold start via boto3 SecretsManager.GetSecretValue. SES sending identity and from-address in Lambda environment variables (not sensitive).
security.abuse_prevention: Approval token is a UUID stored in right_sizing_recommendations.approval_token with single-use flag. On first approval click, approval_lambda sets token_used=true; subsequent calls with same token return 409. Approval tokens expire after 7 days.

data.entities: cost_reports (id, report_date, aws_account_id, total_spend_usd, lending_workload_spend_usd, generated_at), resource_utilization_snapshots (id, resource_id, resource_type ENUM{rds,ec2}, instance_class, region, analysis_date, cpu_p50, cpu_p95, memory_p50_mb, db_connections_p95, error_flag, snapshot_at), right_sizing_recommendations (id, resource_id, resource_type, current_instance_class, recommended_instance_class, projected_monthly_savings_usd, confidence_score, status ENUM{active,approved,actioned,superseded,dismissed}, approval_token, token_used, generated_at, superseded_at), savings_projections (id, recommendation_id FK, current_hourly_usd, recommended_hourly_usd, monthly_delta_usd, pricing_source, projected_at), approval_records (id, recommendation_id FK, approved_by_email, approved_at, resize_outcome ENUM{success,failure,skipped}, resize_completed_at, ssm_execution_id), workload_tags (tag_key, tag_value, account_id, resource_scope).
data.retention_and_deletion: resource_utilization_snapshots deleted after 13 months via Postgres cron job (pg_cron). cost_reports retained 13 months. right_sizing_recommendations retained 2 years. approval_records retained indefinitely.
data.analytics_events: Weekly digest includes: total_recommendations_count, top_10_by_savings, cumulative_savings_realised_ytd_usd computed from approved+actioned recommendations.

devops.deployment_topology: AWS SAM template defines: analysis_lambda (1GB memory, 900s timeout, EventBridge daily rule), digest_lambda (512MB, 60s timeout, EventBridge weekly rule), approval_lambda (256MB, 30s timeout, API Gateway HTTP API trigger), RDS Postgres (t4g.micro, single-AZ), Secrets Manager secret for DB credentials.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: ruff lint, mypy, pytest (unit tests with mocked boto3 via moto library, integration tests against local Postgres via docker-compose), sam build + sam package + sam deploy to dev then production.
devops.rollback_strategy: SAM deploy --no-fail-on-empty-changeset. On Lambda error rate >5% alarm, CloudWatch triggers SNS notification to FinOps team for manual rollback via sam deploy of previous version tag.

ISS-001.title: Lambda timeout risk for large resource inventory
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan analysed all 500 lending workload resources sequentially in a single Lambda invocation. At 300ms per CloudWatch API call this takes 150 seconds for metric fetch alone, risking 15-minute timeout during peak account growth.

ISS-002.title: Approval token single-use enforcement missing
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 did not specify that approval tokens must be single-use. Replaying an approval link after resize could trigger duplicate SSM automation runs on the same resource.

focus_issues.title: Lambda timeout risk for large resource inventory
focus_issues.detail: Sequential CloudWatch calls for 500 resources risks Lambda 15-minute timeout.

focus_issues.title: Approval token single-use enforcement missing
focus_issues.detail: No idempotency guard on approval link prevents duplicate resize automation.

latest_recommendations: Add concurrent CloudWatch API calls using ThreadPoolExecutor with batches of 100 resources to stay within Lambda timeout
Implement single-use approval token with token_used flag in right_sizing_recommendations table and 409 response on replay
revision_memory.latest_plan_title: Lending Workload Cloud Cost Optimiser (Round 1)

convergence_state.reason: Medium timeout risk and low approval idempotency gap need addressing

previous_audits.summary: Round 1 delivered a solid analysis and recommendation loop but left a medium timeout risk for large resource inventories and a low-severity approval idempotency gap.
strengths: IAM least-privilege design correctly separates read and resize roles
pandas p50/p95 utilisation percentile approach well-specified
Postgres schema for recommendations, snapshots, and approval records is comprehensive
concerns: Sequential CloudWatch API calls could timeout for 500+ resource inventory
Approval token replay not guarded against
recommendations: Add ThreadPoolExecutor concurrent CloudWatch calls in UtilisationAnalyser with batch size 100
Add token_used boolean field to right_sizing_recommendations and enforce single-use in approval_lambda

issue_updates.title: Lambda timeout risk
issue_updates.detail: Sequential CloudWatch calls not addressed in Round 1

issue_updates.title: Approval token single-use
issue_updates.detail: No token_used field or idempotency check in approval flow

previous_plan.title: Lending Workload Cloud Cost Optimiser (Round 1)
previous_plan.executive_summary: Daily Lambda analysing AWS Cost Explorer and CloudWatch metrics for lending workloads, generating right-sizing recommendations stored in Postgres, with weekly SES digest and one-click SSM approval-to-resize workflow.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Two Lambda functions (daily analysis, weekly digest) plus approval Lambda triggered by API Gateway. Postgres on RDS t4g.micro for recommendation storage.
previous_plan.data_model: cost_reports, resource_utilization_snapshots, right_sizing_recommendations (missing token_used field), savings_projections, approval_records, workload_tags.
previous_plan.workflows: Daily analysis Lambda fetches Cost Explorer and CloudWatch metrics sequentially per resource, computes p50/p95 via pandas, writes recommendations to Postgres. Weekly digest sends top 10 via SES.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 addresses Lambda timeout risk (ISS-001) via ThreadPoolExecutor concurrent CloudWatch calls in batches of 100, and adds single-use approval token enforcement (ISS-002) via token_used flag and 409 response on replay. The overall architecture is sound from round 1; these are targeted fixes. Plan quality is intentionally weak -- several sections remain thin as per the distribution target for this entry.

fix_report.action_taken: Added ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=10) concurrent CloudWatch GetMetricData calls in UtilisationAnalyser, processing resources in batches of 100 to respect CloudWatch 50 req/s limit and stay within Lambda memory. Updated workflows section to document concurrent fetch pattern and batch sizing rationale.
fix_report.expected_outcome: 500 resource analysis completes in approximately 35 seconds instead of 150 seconds, well within 15-minute Lambda timeout

fix_report.action_taken: Added token_used boolean field to right_sizing_recommendations data model. Updated approval_lambda logic: on token validation, check token_used=False before proceeding; set token_used=True atomically with approval_records insert using Postgres transaction. Returns 409 if token already used.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Duplicate approval link clicks do not trigger repeat SSM resize automation; idempotency enforced at database transaction level

target_output.title: Lending Workload Cloud Cost Optimiser
target_output.executive_summary: A serverless automation tool using two AWS Lambda functions to analyse daily AWS Cost Explorer spend and CloudWatch utilisation metrics for lending workload RDS and EC2 resources, generate ranked right-sizing recommendations with projected monthly savings, and deliver a weekly SES digest with one-click SSM-backed approval-to-resize workflow. Recommendations and approval records stored in Postgres on RDS.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three Lambda functions scheduled via EventBridge Scheduler: analysis_lambda (daily 06:00 UTC) pulls Cost Explorer and CloudWatch data for resources tagged with lending workload IDs, computes p50/p95 CPU and memory utilisation via pandas with concurrent ThreadPoolExecutor fetches in batches of 100, and writes right_sizing_recommendations to Postgres. digest_lambda (Monday 08:00 UTC) queries top 10 recommendations by projected savings and sends formatted SES email to FinOps distribution list. approval_lambda (API Gateway HTTP API trigger) validates single-use approval token from email link, marks token_used, inserts approval_record, and invokes SSM Automation document to right-size the target resource. All Lambda functions run in VPC private subnets; Postgres credentials from Secrets Manager.
target_output.technology_stack: Python 3.12. boto3 for Cost Explorer GetCostAndUsage, CloudWatch GetMetricData, SSM SendCommand, SES SendEmail, Secrets Manager GetSecretValue. pandas for DataFrame-based p50/p95 percentile computation over 14-day metric windows. psycopg2 for Postgres. AWS SAM for IaC deployment. moto for boto3 mocking in pytest. GitHub Actions CI.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Daily Analysis: Cost Explorer cost report ingestion per resource tag group. CloudWatch metric fetch (CPUUtilization, DatabaseConnections, FreeableMemory) per RDS and EC2 resource with concurrent ThreadPoolExecutor batching. Utilisation percentile computation (p50/p95 over 14-day window). Right-size tier lookup from instance family pricing table. Savings projection generation. Weekly Digest: top 10 recommendations by projected_monthly_savings_usd, HTML email with resource ID, current class, recommended class, projected saving, approval link. Approval-to-Resize: approval token validation with single-use enforcement, SSM Automation document invocation for RDS instance class modification or EC2 right-sizing.
target_output.system_components: analysis_lambda (1GB memory, 900s timeout): UtilisationAnalyser with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=10), SavingsProjector, RecommendationWriter. digest_lambda (512MB, 60s timeout): digest HTML generator, SES client. approval_lambda (256MB, 30s timeout): token validator with Postgres transaction, SSM client scoped to cost-optimisation-eligible tagged resources. RDS Postgres t4g.micro: right_sizing_recommendations, resource_utilization_snapshots, approval_records, cost_reports, workload_tags. EventBridge Scheduler rules for daily and weekly triggers. API Gateway HTTP API for approval link endpoint.
target_output.workflows: Daily Cost Analysis: EventBridge triggers analysis_lambda at 06:00 UTC. Lambda fetches Cost Explorer GetCostAndUsage for lending account tags over prior day, stores cost_reports row. Fetches resource list (EC2 Describe, RDS Describe) filtered by workload_tags. Splits resources into batches of 100; ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=10) sends concurrent CloudWatch GetMetricData calls per batch (respects 50 req/s limit). pandas computes p50/p95 CPU and memory per resource over 14-day window. SavingsProjector looks up current and recommended instance class pricing, computes projected_monthly_savings_usd. RecommendationWriter upserts right_sizing_recommendations, marking prior active recommendations for same resource_id as superseded. Approval-to-Resize: Approver clicks pre-signed API Gateway URL in weekly digest email. approval_lambda extracts recommendation_id and token from URL. Postgres transaction: SELECT right_sizing_recommendations WHERE id=:id AND approval_token=:token AND token_used=False FOR UPDATE. If found, set token_used=True, insert approval_records{approved_at, approved_by_email from Cognito claim}. Call SSM StartAutomationExecution with instance ID and target instance class. On SSM success, update approval_records.resize_outcome=success. On 409 (token already used), return 'This recommendation has already been approved'.
target_output.data_model: cost_reports (id, report_date, aws_account_id, total_spend_usd, lending_workload_spend_usd, generated_at). resource_utilization_snapshots (id, resource_id, resource_type ENUM{rds,ec2}, instance_class, region, analysis_date, cpu_p50, cpu_p95, memory_p50_mb, db_connections_p95, error_flag, snapshot_at). right_sizing_recommendations (id, resource_id, resource_type, current_instance_class, recommended_instance_class, projected_monthly_savings_usd, confidence_score, status ENUM{active,approved,actioned,superseded,dismissed}, approval_token UUID, token_used BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE, generated_at, superseded_at). savings_projections (id, recommendation_id FK, current_hourly_usd, recommended_hourly_usd, monthly_delta_usd, pricing_source, projected_at). approval_records (id, recommendation_id FK, approved_by_email, approved_at, resize_outcome ENUM{success,failure,skipped}, resize_completed_at, ssm_execution_id). workload_tags (tag_key, tag_value, account_id, resource_scope).
target_output.api_design: API Gateway HTTP API: GET /approve?recommendation_id=:id&token=:token (triggers approval_lambda, validates token, initiates SSM resize, returns HTML confirmation page). Internal Lambda invocations: analysis_lambda writes to Postgres directly; digest_lambda reads Postgres and calls SES SendEmail. No REST API for external consumption -- tool is fully automated except for the approval endpoint.
target_output.security_and_compliance: IAM roles: analysis_lambda role has Cost Explorer GetCostAndUsage, CloudWatch GetMetricData, RDS DescribeDBInstances, EC2 DescribeInstances, Secrets Manager GetSecretValue for DB credentials. approval_lambda role has SSM StartAutomationExecution restricted to resources with tag cost-optimisation-eligible=true via IAM condition, RDS ModifyDBInstance for instance class changes only. Postgres credentials in Secrets Manager with 90-day rotation. Approval tokens UUID single-use enforced via Postgres transaction with SELECT FOR UPDATE. API Gateway approval endpoint protected by IAM auth requiring internal AWS credentials (approvers access via pre-signed URL containing SigV4 signature).
target_output.deployment_and_operations: AWS SAM template: three Lambda functions, EventBridge Scheduler rules, API Gateway HTTP API, RDS Postgres t4g.micro, Secrets Manager secret, IAM roles. GitHub Actions pipeline: ruff lint, mypy, pytest with moto-mocked boto3 and docker-compose Postgres for integration tests, sam build, sam deploy to dev account then production account via assume-role. Manual approval gate in GitHub Actions before production deploy.
target_output.observability: CloudWatch Logs for all Lambda functions. CloudWatch Alarm: analysis_lambda error rate >0 triggers SNS email to FinOps team. analysis_lambda duration >600 seconds alarm for timeout early warning. Custom metric: recommendations_generated_count published per analysis run. Weekly digest includes cumulative_savings_realised_ytd_usd from approved+actioned recommendations.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Approximately $15/month. Lambda invocations: 365 daily + 52 weekly analysis runs well within free tier. API Gateway HTTP API near-zero cost at approval frequency. RDS t4g.micro ($13/month). CloudWatch detailed monitoring for 500 resources adds $5/month. Total tool cost of approximately $20/month against estimated $140,000/month savings opportunity at 35% over-provisioning rate.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (weeks 1-4): analysis_lambda with ThreadPoolExecutor concurrent CloudWatch fetches, Postgres schema with all entities, right-sizing recommendations for RDS instances only, weekly SES digest with top 10, approval token single-use enforcement, SAM IaC, GitHub Actions CI. Phase 2 (weeks 5-8): EC2 right-sizing recommendations, SSM Automation document for approved resize execution, approval_lambda with API Gateway, savings realised tracking, historical cost trend reporting.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python ruff and mypy in CI. pytest with moto for boto3 mocking; no real AWS API calls in unit tests. docker-compose Postgres for integration tests. pandas DataFrames documented with column dtypes. IAM policies reviewed in code review for least-privilege. No hardcoded resource IDs or account IDs -- all from workload_tags table or environment variables.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: CloudWatch detailed monitoring must be pre-enabled on target resources; if not enabled, GetMetricData returns insufficient data and recommendation quality degrades. Mitigation: analysis_lambda checks monitoring state and sets error_flag on snapshots lacking sufficient data points. Risk: SSM Automation resize of RDS instance causes brief downtime during instance class change. Mitigation: approval email includes estimated downtime warning; approver confirms during maintenance window. Tradeoff: single-use token via Postgres transaction over a dedicated token service reduces dependencies but requires Postgres availability for approval flow.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: ThreadPoolExecutor max_workers=10 confirmed sufficient for 500 resources in batches of 100 within Lambda 15-minute timeout. Single-use approval token implemented via Postgres SELECT FOR UPDATE transaction rather than Redis to avoid adding cache infrastructure. SAM chosen over CDK for IaC due to single-developer maintenance preference. RDS t4g.micro confirmed adequate for low-volume internal tool writes.

metadata.notes: Round 2 cloud cost optimisation Lambda tool; fixes Lambda timeout concurrency and approval token idempotency.

profile.domain: Bank Wholesale Deposit Rate Board

project_goal.value: Static informational website publishing daily term deposit and money market rates to corporate treasury clients, handling traffic spikes during volatile rate environments
project_goal.rationale: Treasury clients need a reliable, fast-loading rate board that stays available when rates move sharply and traffic spikes

target_users.value: Corporate treasury clients and institutional depositors checking wholesale deposit rates daily
target_users.rationale: Sophisticated institutional users checking rates via desktop browser; no authentication required

project_class.rationale: Fully static Hugo site; no server-side runtime required; all content generated at build time from data files

capabilities.rationale: Static frontend pages and Google Analytics for traffic and rate page engagement metrics

complexity_level.rationale: Hugo static site with JSON data files and GitHub Actions build pipeline; no dynamic backend

risk_level.rationale: Public informational site with no user data collection; main risk is CDN availability during rate volatility spikes

data_sensitivity.rationale: Rate data is public information; no user PII collected

external_exposure.rationale: Publicly accessible by any institutional client without login

access_model.value: Public read-only; no authentication required for rate viewing
access_model.rationale: Wholesale rates are publicly disclosed under FDIC rate disclosure requirements

feature_scope.value: Current day rate card by product tier, historical rate archive pages auto-generated from data files, CSV download of historical rates, rate publication timestamp and FDIC disclaimer, CDN-cached global delivery
feature_scope.rationale: Core institutional client needs: current rates, history download, compliance disclosures

mvp_scope.value: Current rate card, publication timestamp, FDIC disclaimer, GitHub Actions daily build and CDN cache invalidation
mvp_scope.rationale: Rate display with mandatory disclosures is minimum viable for FDIC compliance

security_baseline.value: HTTPS enforced via Cloud CDN SSL certificate; no user input forms; Content-Security-Policy header disabling inline scripts
security_baseline.rationale: Static site with no forms or dynamic content; CSP reduces clickjacking and XSS risk on rate pages

frontend_stack.value: Hugo static site generator with Tailwind CSS
frontend_stack.rationale: Hugo builds fast; Tailwind for utility-first styling; no JavaScript framework needed for static rate pages

backend_stack.value: none
backend_stack.rationale: static_website; no server-side runtime

data_platform.value: none
data_platform.rationale: Rate data stored as JSON files in Git repository; no database required

hosting_target.value: GCP Cloud Storage + Cloud CDN with global anycast edge caching
hosting_target.rationale: Cloud Storage for static hosting; Cloud CDN for low-latency global delivery and spike traffic absorption

privacy_retention_policy.value: No user data collected; Google Analytics uses anonymous session IDs with IP anonymisation enabled; analytics data retained 14 months per GA default
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: No PII collected; GA IP anonymisation satisfies minimal privacy obligation

future_scope.value: Rate alert subscription (email notification when rates change), interactive rate comparison chart for institutional clients
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 enhancements require adding a Firestore-backed subscription service (separate project)

constraints.value: Rate publication must complete within 3 minutes of treasury team repo commit; FDIC rate disclosure requires publication timestamp and rate disclaimer on every rate page
constraints.rationale: Treasury clients expect rates to be live within minutes of bank publication; FDIC mandates visible timestamps

observability_baseline.value: Google Cloud Monitoring for Cloud CDN cache hit ratio and origin request rate; GitHub Actions build duration alerts; Uptime Check on rate board URL
observability_baseline.rationale: CDN cache hit ratio is the primary reliability indicator; uptime check ensures pages serve during spike traffic

execution_preference.value: Treasury operations team self-service via Git commit to rates data file; no engineering involvement for daily rate updates
execution_preference.rationale: Treasury staff must update rates independently without developer bottleneck

project.goal: Static wholesale deposit rate board with CDN-cached global delivery
project.target_users: Corporate treasury clients

devops.ci: GitHub Actions on push to main

reasoner_reviews.product: The core use case is simple: treasury operations commits a JSON rate file, GitHub Actions builds Hugo and invalidates CDN cache, clients see updated rates within 3 minutes. The historical archive pages auto-generated from data files are a nice differentiator versus a manual PDF rate sheet. FDIC timestamp and disclaimer are non-negotiable on every rate page.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Hugo is well-suited for this use case -- sub-second builds for a rate board site with a few hundred data-driven pages. Cloud CDN with global anycast provides the spike traffic absorption needed during rate volatility events. The 3-minute publication window is achievable: Hugo build <30 seconds, GCS sync <30 seconds, CDN cache invalidation <60 seconds.
reasoner_reviews.security: Content-Security-Policy must disallow inline scripts to prevent clickjacking on rate pages. The FDIC rate disclaimer text must be rendered server-side by Hugo (in the template), not injected client-side via JavaScript, to ensure it is always present in the HTML source. No user input forms means XSS attack surface is near-zero.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Cloud CDN cache invalidation is the critical path for the 3-minute window. GCP Cloud CDN invalidation typically completes in 60 seconds globally. Recommend setting Hugo output cache headers to max-age=300 (5 minutes) on rate pages so stale rates are served for at most 5 minutes if invalidation is delayed.

frontend.app_structure: Hugo site with layouts/: base.html (FDIC disclaimer footer), rate-card.html (current day rates table by product tier), archive.html (historical rates for a given date), index.html (landing with today's rates summary). Data in /data/rates/YYYY-MM-DD.json files. Hugo range over data files generates archive pages automatically.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Home / (today's rate card with publication timestamp). /rates/YYYY-MM-DD/ (archive page per date, auto-generated). /download/ (CSV download of historical rates, generated from /data/rates/ files). Each page includes FDIC disclaimer footer with publication_date from front matter.
frontend.performance_notes: Hugo builds sub-30 seconds for up to 5 years of daily rate archive pages (approximately 1,800 pages). Tailwind CSS purged at build time -- CSS bundle under 15KB. Cloud CDN serves pages with cache-control: max-age=300. No JavaScript on rate pages except GA tag.

security.auth_design: No authentication. Public read-only static site.
security.abuse_prevention: Cloud Armor WAF rule: rate-limit to 1,000 req/min per IP to prevent scraping abuse during rate volatility events. No user forms to protect.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Cloud CDN access logs to Cloud Logging for traffic analytics. GitHub Actions audit trail for every rate file commit.

devops.deployment_topology: GCP Cloud Storage bucket (rates.bank.com) with website hosting enabled. Cloud CDN load balancer frontend with SSL certificate from Google-managed certificate. Cloud Armor WAF policy attached to load balancer for rate limiting. Custom domain CNAME to CDN IP. GitHub Actions workflow: hugo build, gsutil rsync to GCS bucket, gcloud compute url-maps invalidate-cdn-cache for /rates/* and /.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions on push to main: hugo --minify (sub-30 second build), gsutil -m rsync -r -d public/ gs://rates.bank.com/ (parallel GCS sync), gcloud compute url-maps invalidate-cdn-cache --path '/*' (CDN cache invalidation). Total workflow target: under 3 minutes. On failure: Slack webhook notification to treasury-ops channel.
devops.observability_stack: Google Cloud Monitoring dashboard: Cloud CDN cache hit ratio (target >95%), origin request rate, CDN 5xx error rate. Uptime Check on https://rates.bank.com/ from us-central1 and europe-west1 with 60-second check interval. Alert: CDN cache hit ratio <80% or uptime check failure triggers PagerDuty.

data.entities: rate_cards: JSON file per date at /data/rates/YYYY-MM-DD.json with fields: publication_date, published_by, product_tiers (array of {product_name, term_days, rate_bps, minimum_deposit_usd, fdic_disclaimer_text}). product_tiers: Hugo data template iterating rate_cards.product_tiers. publication_dates: derived from /data/rates/ directory listing for archive page generation. historical_rate_archives: Hugo generates /rates/YYYY-MM-DD/ pages from all files in /data/rates/.
data.schema_notes: rate_bps stored as integer basis points in JSON (e.g., 525 = 5.25%) and formatted to percentage string in Hugo template to avoid floating-point display issues. FDIC disclaimer text embedded in each rate JSON file to ensure it matches the rate publication exactly.
data.data_risks: If treasury operations commits a malformed JSON rate file, Hugo build fails and CDN retains prior day rates. GitHub Actions build failure must notify treasury-ops Slack channel immediately so staff can correct the file.

ISS-001.title: FDIC disclaimer missing from archive pages in Round 1
